candidates.name: Michael
candidates.one_line: Field-marshal archangel who threw the Dragon out of Heaven — the house's head of security, sword always within reach of the suit.

candidates.name: Gabriel
candidates.one_line: The announcement angel — every world-changing message in three religions went through this one press office.

candidates.name: Raphael
candidates.one_line: Healer archangel of the Book of Tobit who binds demons and travels incognito as a hired guide.

candidates.name: Uriel
candidates.one_line: Angel of the fire of God, keeper of Eden's flaming sword in apocryphal tradition — the quiet one holding the light.

candidates.name: Azrael
candidates.one_line: The angel of death as courteous functionary — a ledger of every name, and he always collects on schedule.

candidates.name: Metatron
candidates.one_line: The scribe seated closest to the throne, 'lesser YHWH' of the Hekhalot texts — heaven's chief of staff who was once mortal (Enoch cycle, story-only).

candidates.name: Sandalphon
candidates.one_line: Metatron's towering twin who weaves prayers into crowns — mailroom of the cosmos, five hundred years tall.

candidates.name: Raziel
candidates.one_line: Keeper of the Book of Secrets containing all celestial knowledge — the angel who leaked the manual to Adam.

candidates.name: Samael
candidates.one_line: The venom-of-God accuser who is somehow still on heaven's payroll — prosecutor, poisoner, and consort of Lilith.

candidates.name: Lucifer
candidates.one_line: The morning star who bet everything against the house once and lost — the original all-in, still playing for a comeback.

candidates.name: Beelzebub
candidates.one_line: Lord of the Flies, a demoted Philistine god turned prince of demons — old money gone rancid.

candidates.name: Asmodeus
candidates.one_line: Demon king of lust and wrath whom Solomon shackled into construction labor — knows every trick because he built the table.

candidates.name: Lilith
candidates.one_line: Adam's first wife who refused to lie beneath and flew — night-demon queen, and lately a AAA-game headliner (Diablo IV).

candidates.name: Belial
candidates.one_line: The worthless one, prince of the Sons of Darkness in the Dead Sea Scrolls — smooth, lawless, and impossible to pin to a contract.

candidates.name: Astaroth
candidates.one_line: Goetic grand duke riding a dragon with a viper in hand — a fallen goddess (Astarte) filed under a demon's name.

candidates.name: Paimon
candidates.one_line: Goetic king who arrives with a deafening orchestra and teaches all arts and sciences — horror cinema's favorite name (Hereditary).

candidates.name: Mammon
candidates.one_line: Wealth personified into a demon — the only fiend every casino already worships without knowing it.

candidates.name: Abaddon
candidates.one_line: Angel of the bottomless pit whose name IS destruction — the locust-king who opens the floor when the game ends.

candidates.name: Baphomet
candidates.one_line: The goat-headed idol from Templar confessions, redrawn by Lévi into occultism's most famous icon — a symbol that accreted a self.

candidates.name: Mephistopheles
candidates.one_line: The contract-devil of the Faust legend — folklore's finest closer, always reading the fine print you didn't.

candidates.name: Azazel
candidates.one_line: The scapegoat-wilderness demon and fallen Watcher who taught humans weapons and cosmetics, bound under rocks until judgment — nothing left to lose.

candidates.name: Semyaza
candidates.one_line: Leader of the two hundred Watchers who swore a mutual oath on Mount Hermon and fell together — a syndicate that went bust as one.

candidates.name: Stolas
candidates.one_line: Crowned owl-prince of the Goetia who teaches astronomy and the virtue of herbs; the goetic original is public domain, the popular animated version is not.

candidates.name: Bael
candidates.one_line: First king of the Goetia, three heads (man, cat, toad), grants invisibility — the demoted storm-god at the top of the ledger.

candidates.name: Leviathan
candidates.one_line: The sea-monster God made to play with, whose flesh is reserved for the banquet at the end of days — the ocean as an opponent.

candidates.name: Behemoth
candidates.one_line: The land-titan of the Book of Job, Leviathan's terrestrial counterpart — unkillable except by its Maker.

candidates.name: Ziz
candidates.one_line: The griffin-bird of rabbinic legend whose wingspan blots out the sun — third of the great banquet beasts, air to Leviathan's sea and Behemoth's land.

candidates.name: Ghul (ghoul)
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting graveyard haunter of Arabian folk tradition that lures travelers off the road — the loanword every horror game borrowed.

candidates.name: Nephilim
candidates.one_line: The giants born of Watchers and human women — the mighty men of old whose drowned ghosts became the demons of folk demonology.

candidates.name: Jinn (folk class)
candidates.one_line: Smokeless-fire beings living parallel lives beside humanity — an entire hidden society of gamblers, grudges, and granted wishes.

candidates.name: Ifrit
candidates.one_line: The strongest and most cunning tier of jinn, fire-wreathed and proud — a fantasy-games summon staple.

candidates.name: Marid
candidates.one_line: Rebellious sea-jinn of great power, the wish-granting genie archetype of the 1001 Nights bottle stories.

candidates.name: Dybbuk
candidates.one_line: A restless dead soul that clings inside a living host — Yiddish folklore's possession engine, now a horror-film regular.

candidates.name: Shedim
candidates.one_line: Talmudic demons with rooster feet who eat, drink, breed, and die like us — the mundane demon underclass of Jewish folklore.

candidates.name: Golem
candidates.one_line: Clay servant animated by the written Name and unmade by erasing one letter — the being whose off-switch is an edit.

candidates.name: Santa Muerte
candidates.one_line: The skeletal Lady of Holy Death, 10-12 million living devotees and Vatican condemnation — the fastest-growing cult in the Americas.

candidates.name: San La Muerte
candidates.one_line: Argentina and Paraguay's own skeletal folk saint, carved in bone and carried under the skin for protection.

candidates.name: Maximón (San Simón)
candidates.one_line: Cigar-smoking, liquor-drinking Maya-Catholic trickster saint kept under 24/7 cofradía guard in Santiago Atitlán — petitioned for fortune and favors.

candidates.name: Krampus
candidates.one_line: The chain-rattling horned companion who punishes where the saint rewards — Alpine folk Christianity's enforcer, now a holiday-horror star.

candidates.name: Gad
candidates.one_line: Pan-Semitic god of luck for whom apostate Judah 'set a table' (Isaiah 65:11) — a fortune-god condemned by name, made for casino work.

candidates.name: Meni
candidates.one_line: Gad's partner deity of destiny and allotment, toasted with mixed wine — fate as the second croupier.

candidates.name: Seal of Solomon
candidates.one_line: The signet ring that bound demon kings to build a temple — command authority over the entire fiend roster, in jewelry form.

candidates.name: Shamir
candidates.one_line: The worm (or stone) that cuts any material by its gaze alone, used to hew temple stones without iron — kept wrapped in wool in a lead box.

candidates.name: Holy Grail
candidates.one_line: The ultimate quest-object of medieval romance — sacred-core adjacent in origin, but its game-famous form is the public-domain Arthurian literary cycle.

candidates.name: Spear of Longinus
candidates.one_line: The lance of the Crucifixion turned world-conquest talisman in medieval and modern legend — sacred-core adjacent, owner review advised.

closed_material_notes: HARD-EXCLUDED (sacred core): God/YHWH/Allah, Jesus, Muhammad, Moses, Abraham, and all prophets — never candidates in any class. ISLAMIC NO-DEPICTION RULE: named Islamic sacred figures excluded even where mythologically rich — Iblis (the refuser), the Buraq (night-journey steed), Harut and Marut (Babylon angels), houris; jinn as an unnamed folk class are the only Islamic-adjacent material listed, and all carry 'living'. D-4 HISTORICAL HUMANS excluded: Solomon the person (his binding-of-demons cycle is listed via Seal of Solomon/Asmodeus/Shamir as story material); Rabbi Judah Loew of Prague (golem-maker; golem itself listed); St. Nicholas of Myra and all canonical saints (real people — their legend cycles are story-only material); folk saints who were real or plausibly-real people: Gauchito Gil, Jesús Malverde, Niño Fidencio (their cults may be mentioned as event/world color only). Santa Muerte, San La Muerte, and Maximón ARE listed because they are personifications/syncretic composites, not historical individuals — but all three are actively worshipped and owner-gated. EXCLUDED AS HARMFUL TROPE: the Wandering Jew — a medieval antisemitic legend; do not use. RESTRICTED PRACTICE: Maximón's cofradía ritual specifics (Santiago Atitlán) are community-held; only widely-published public-sphere material is represented, and the entry carries 'restricted'. SACRED-CORE-ADJACENT ARTIFACTS flagged rather than excluded: Holy Grail and Spear of Longinus listed via their public-domain literary cycles; the Ark of the Covenant and Passion relics as sacred objects proper were NOT listed — owner call. Kabbalistic sefirot/divine-name material treated as sacred core, not censused.

candidates.name: King Arthur
candidates.one_line: The once and future king — sleeping in Avalon until Britain's darkest hour, crown pawned to prophecy itself.

candidates.name: Merlin
candidates.one_line: Demon-fathered prophet-enchanter who built a king and was unmade by his own student, sealed alive under stone.

candidates.name: Morgan le Fay
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting sorceress-queen of Avalon, Arthur's half-sister, healer and destroyer in one — and the one figure here with a modern shrine.

candidates.name: Guinevere
candidates.one_line: The queen whose love triangle burned Camelot to the ground.

candidates.name: Lancelot
candidates.one_line: The greatest knight alive, whose one flaw was loving the one woman forbidden to him.

candidates.name: Gawain
candidates.one_line: Sun-strengthened knight of courtesy who kept a beheading appointment with his own executioner.

candidates.name: Galahad
candidates.one_line: The sinless knight who achieved the Grail and was raptured out of the story mid-triumph.

candidates.name: Percival
candidates.one_line: The holy fool raised in the woods who blundered his way to the Grail castle.

candidates.name: Tristan
candidates.one_line: Harp-playing knight doomed by a love potion drunk by mistake.

candidates.name: Isolde
candidates.one_line: The Irish princess-healer bound to Tristan by a potion meant for her husband.

candidates.name: Mordred
candidates.one_line: Arthur's incest-born son and traitor, who killed his father while dying on his spear.

candidates.name: Morgause
candidates.one_line: Queen of Orkney, mother of Mordred by her own half-brother — the dynasty's poisoned root.

candidates.name: Uther Pendragon
candidates.one_line: The dragon-king who took another man's shape and wife to sire Arthur.

candidates.name: Sir Kay
candidates.one_line: Arthur's sharp-tongued foster brother and seneschal — in the oldest Welsh tales a fire-breathing superhuman.

candidates.name: Sir Bedivere
candidates.one_line: One-handed marshal who twice lied about throwing Excalibur back before finally letting go.

candidates.name: Lady of the Lake (Nimue/Viviane)
candidates.one_line: Water-fay arms dealer of destiny — hands out Excalibur, raises Lancelot, entombs Merlin.

candidates.name: The Fisher King
candidates.one_line: Wounded keeper of the Grail whose unhealing injury blights his whole land — a ruler with nothing left to lose but the question that saves him.

candidates.name: The Green Knight
candidates.one_line: Holly-bearing giant who stakes his own head on a Christmas wager and picks it up when it's lopped off.

candidates.name: The Questing Beast
candidates.one_line: Serpent-leopard-lion-hart chimera whose belly bays like thirty hounds — hunted forever, caught never.

candidates.name: Cath Palug
candidates.one_line: Monstrous cat of Anglesey that slaughtered nine-score warriors before Kay put it down.

candidates.name: Twrch Trwyth
candidates.one_line: A cursed king turned colossal boar, hunted across Wales for the comb and shears between his ears.

candidates.name: Giant of Mont-Saint-Michel
candidates.one_line: Man-eating cloak-of-beards giant Arthur kills single-handed on the tidal mount.

candidates.name: Excalibur
candidates.one_line: The lake-given blade whose scabbard stops all bleeding — thrown back to the water at the story's end.

candidates.name: The Holy Grail
candidates.one_line: The ultimate unattainable prize — cup of the Last Supper, achieved only by the perfect and ruinous to everyone else.

candidates.name: The Round Table
candidates.one_line: A table with no head, one lethal empty seat, and the world's most famous membership roster.

candidates.name: Robin Hood
candidates.one_line: Greenwood outlaw-archer who robs the rich, splits arrows, and wins every archery wager he enters.

candidates.name: Maid Marian
candidates.one_line: May-games queen of the greenwood, as handy with a sword as with a disguise.

candidates.name: Little John
candidates.one_line: Seven-foot quarterstaff man who dunked Robin Hood at their first meeting and never left his side.

candidates.name: Friar Tuck
candidates.one_line: The fighting friar — fat, jolly, and fully capable of throwing you in the river.

candidates.name: Sheriff of Nottingham
candidates.one_line: The archetypal corrupt lawman, forever rigging archery contests he's doomed to lose.

candidates.name: Melusine
candidates.one_line: Serpent-tailed fairy bride and dynasty-founder who flies shrieking from the tower when her secret is seen.

candidates.name: Prester John
candidates.one_line: Phantom priest-king of a fabulously wealthy eastern realm that all Europe hunted and no one ever found.

candidates.name: Pied Piper of Hamelin
candidates.one_line: The rainbow-coated ratcatcher who, stiffed on his fee, piped away a town's children — never welsh on a debt.

candidates.name: Herne the Hunter
candidates.one_line: Antlered ghost of Windsor Forest, rattling chains and blasting oaks where he hanged himself.

candidates.name: Tam Lin
candidates.one_line: A knight stolen by the Fairy Queen as her tithe to Hell, won back by a lover who wouldn't let go through every monstrous shape.

candidates.name: King Herla
candidates.one_line: A king who left a dwarf's wedding to find centuries gone — now rides the Wild Hunt, unable to dismount without crumbling to dust.

candidates.name: Ogier the Dane
candidates.one_line: Legendary Danish champion asleep beneath Kronborg Castle, beard grown through the table, waiting for the realm's peril.

candidates.name: Lohengrin
candidates.one_line: The Swan Knight who champions a lady on one condition — never ask his name — and vanishes when she does.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this family is culturally closed or restricted — all core texts are public, published, public-domain literature. D-4 EXCLUSIONS (verified historical humans whose legend cycles are story-only material): Charlemagne and Roland (Roland = the historical Hruodland of the 778 Roncevaux ambush; the Song of Roland and the wider Matter of France remain rich story-only material — Durendal the unbreakable sword could resurface later as a stand-alone artifact candidate if the owner rules sword-of-a-historical-owner admissible); El Cid (Rodrigo Díaz de Vivar, Cantar de mio Cid); Dietrich von Bern (legendary mask of the historical Theodoric the Great — Dietrich cycle is story-only); Tannhauser (historical minnesinger; the Venusberg legend is story-only); Lady Godiva (historical countess); Hereward the Wake (historical resistance leader). Borderline judged IN as legend-first: Robin Hood (no verified historical individual behind the ballads), Ogier the Dane and Prester John (no historical persons at all), Melusine (pure fairy legend despite Lusignan dynastic attachment). Judged OUT but not for D-4: the Wandering Jew legend is excluded on sensitivity grounds — a medieval legend with a long antisemitic deployment history, unsuitable for casino-content use; William Tell was left off as disputed-historicity Swiss national material better handled under an Alpine/Germanic folk tradition if one exists. Sensitivity carve-out inside the list: the Holy Grail is retained (recognition 5, indispensable) but should be owner-reviewed before any irreverent gambling framing, as it doubles as a living-religion Christian symbol.

candidates.name: Avalokiteshvara (Guanyin / Kannon)
candidates.one_line: Thousand-armed bodhisattva of compassion who hears every cry in every realm — the most-worshipped figure in East Asia.

candidates.name: Tara
candidates.one_line: Swift green-and-white savioress born from a tear of compassion, rescuer from the eight great fears.

candidates.name: Manjushri
candidates.one_line: Bodhisattva of wisdom wielding the flaming sword that cuts through ignorance.

candidates.name: Ksitigarbha (Jizo / Dizang)
candidates.one_line: Bodhisattva who vowed to empty every hell before accepting his own buddhahood — guardian of dead children and travelers.

candidates.name: Maitreya
candidates.one_line: The buddha-to-come, waiting in Tushita heaven for a far-future age — patron of every long game.

candidates.name: Amitabha
candidates.one_line: Buddha of Infinite Light whose Pure Land admits anyone who sincerely calls his name — the biggest open-door house in the cosmos.

candidates.name: Vairocana
candidates.one_line: The cosmic sun-buddha whose body is the universe itself; the Great Buddha of Nara is his portrait.

candidates.name: Bhaisajyaguru (Medicine Buddha)
candidates.one_line: Lapis-lazuli buddha of healing whose twelve vows cure sickness of body and karma.

candidates.name: Vajrapani
candidates.one_line: Thunderbolt-wielding protector who stood bodyguard over the Buddha — wrath in service of restraint.

candidates.name: Yama (Enma / Yanluo)
candidates.one_line: King and judge of the hells who weighs every soul's ledger — death as an audit, not a raid.

candidates.name: Vaisravana (Bishamonten)
candidates.one_line: Heavenly King of the North and god of wealth — counted among Japan's Seven Lucky Gods, a mongoose spitting jewels at his side.

candidates.name: Jambhala (Dzambhala)
candidates.one_line: Pot-bellied Tibetan wealth deity in five colors who bestows prosperity so practitioners can stop worrying about money.

candidates.name: Vasudhara
candidates.one_line: Golden goddess of abundance streaming grain and gems from her hands — Nepal's favorite household fortune deity.

candidates.name: Benzaiten (Buddhist Sarasvati)
candidates.one_line: Lute-playing goddess of eloquence, music, and fortune — a Seven Lucky Gods regular enshrined on islands across Japan.

candidates.name: Shakra / Indra (Taishakuten)
candidates.one_line: King of the Thirty-Three Gods who repeatedly tests mortals in disguise — and who dreads the five signs of his own divine decay.

candidates.name: Brahma Sahampati
candidates.one_line: The high god who descended to beg the newly awakened Buddha to teach — heaven petitioning a mortal.

candidates.name: Mahakala
candidates.one_line: Black wrathful Great Time, chief dharmapala — his white form is invoked specifically for wealth.

candidates.name: Palden Lhamo
candidates.one_line: The only goddess among Tibet's eight great protectors, riding a mule across a sea of blood, dice hanging from her saddle to divine fates.

candidates.name: Hariti (Kishimojin)
candidates.one_line: Child-devouring demoness converted by the Buddha into the fierce patron goddess of children and mothers.

candidates.name: Skanda (Weituo)
candidates.one_line: Swift armored general of the devas who chased down the demon that stole the Buddha's relic tooth — guardian of monasteries.

candidates.name: Marici
candidates.one_line: Goddess of the dawn ray who cannot be seen, seized, or bound — invoked by warriors for invisibility in battle.

candidates.name: Acala (Fudo Myoo)
candidates.one_line: The Immovable Wisdom King, sword and lasso in a curtain of flame — Japan's favorite unmovable object.

candidates.name: Yamantaka (Vajrabhairava)
candidates.one_line: The buffalo-headed 'Slayer of Death' — the wisdom that terrifies Yama himself.

candidates.name: Hayagriva
candidates.one_line: Horse-headed wrathful king whose neigh shatters obstacles and demons.

candidates.name: Mara
candidates.one_line: Lord of desire and death who staked everything on breaking the Buddha's resolve — armies, daughters, doubt — and lost the wager of the age.

candidates.name: Asura
candidates.one_line: Jealous titan-gods cast down from Mount Meru, doomed to endless envious war against the devas above.

candidates.name: Preta (hungry ghost)
candidates.one_line: Needle-throated ghosts with mountain bellies — beings bankrupted by their own greed, never able to swallow what they crave.

candidates.name: Rakshasa
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting man-eating demons of the wilds, some converted into fierce guardians of the Lotus Sutra.

candidates.name: Naga
candidates.one_line: Serpent kings of the waters and treasure-hoards beneath the earth, keepers of hidden sutras.

candidates.name: Garuda
candidates.one_line: Colossal golden bird, eternal devourer of nagas — wingspan measured in miles.

candidates.name: Mucalinda
candidates.one_line: The naga king who coiled seven times around the meditating Buddha and spread his hood against the storm.

candidates.name: Yaksha
candidates.one_line: Nature spirits of wealth-guarding ambivalence — shrine-keepers, treasure-wardens, occasional man-eaters.

candidates.name: Apsara
candidates.one_line: Celestial dancers of the deva courts, carved by the thousand across Angkor's walls.

candidates.name: Gandharva
candidates.one_line: Fragrance-eating celestial musicians who play for the gods between the realms.

candidates.name: Kinnara
candidates.one_line: Half-bird, half-human celestial lovers famed for devotion and song.

candidates.name: Dakini
candidates.one_line: Sky-dancing wisdom women, fierce and unpredictable — initiators who test practitioners at the edge.

candidates.name: Sudhana (Shancai)
candidates.one_line: The pilgrim boy of the Gandavyuha who sought out fifty-three teachers — the archetypal seeker, later Guanyin's acolyte.

candidates.name: Cintamani
candidates.one_line: The wish-fulfilling jewel held by bodhisattvas and naga kings — grants whatever is asked, revealing what the asker truly is.

candidates.name: Vajra
candidates.one_line: The diamond-thunderbolt scepter — indestructible, irresistible, the signature weapon-symbol of an entire vehicle of Buddhism.

closed_material_notes: EXCLUDED PER D-4 AND FOUNDER RESTRICTION: (1) The historical Buddha (Siddhartha Gautama) — founder of a living major religion, never a candidate in any form, including his cosmic epithets when they denote the person. (2) The Jataka tales (547 previous lives of the Buddha, incl. Prince Vessantara) are legally public-domain in translation but are lives OF the founder — story-only material, no entity extraction. (3) Historical/deified humans excluded as entities, legend cycles usable as story-only material: Padmasambhava (Guru Rinpoche — also actively venerated, doubly gated), Milarepa, Bodhidharma (Daruma), Budai (the 'Laughing Buddha' — a 10th-c. Chinese monk, not the Buddha and not a deity candidate), Guan Yu in his Buddhist Sangharama-protector role, and the historical disciples/arhats (Ananda, Mahakasyapa, Maudgalyayana — whose Mulian-rescues-his-mother hell-journey is rich story-only material; Angulimala the converted killer likewise). (4) TRADITION-RESTRICTED, NOT COLLECTED: anuttarayoga tantra deity practices (Kalachakra, Cakrasamvara, Hevajra, Guhyasamaja, Vajrayogini) require formal empowerment per all Tibetan lineages — public museum iconography exists, but the practice content is initiation-gated, so these are reported here rather than listed; Yamantaka and the dakini class ARE listed above via widely published public-sphere material with flag restricted for owner review. (5) Dorje Shugden excluded entirely — subject of an active, painful sectarian controversy within living Tibetan Buddhism; no game use.

candidates.name: The Morrígan
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting crow goddess of war and fate who chooses who falls on the battlefield.

candidates.name: Lugh
candidates.one_line: The many-skilled shining one who talked his way into the gods' hall by mastering every art — credited inventor of the board game fidchell and founder of funeral games of skill.

candidates.name: Brigid
candidates.one_line: Triple goddess of poetry, healing, and the forge whose flame is still tended — folded into the living cult of St. Brigid.

candidates.name: The Dagda
candidates.one_line: Club-dragging father-god whose cauldron leaves no company unsatisfied and whose harp orders the seasons.

candidates.name: Manannán mac Lir
candidates.one_line: Sea-lord in a mist cloak with a boat that obeys thought and a crane-bag of treasures.

candidates.name: Nuada Airgetlám
candidates.one_line: Silver-armed king unmade by a maiming — lost his throne with his hand and clawed both back.

candidates.name: Danu
candidates.one_line: Shadowy ancestral mother who gives the Tuatha Dé Danann their name and little else that survives.

candidates.name: Aengus
candidates.one_line: Young god of love who won his father's bright hall through a lawyer's trick of 'a day and a night'.

candidates.name: Goibniu
candidates.one_line: Divine smith whose spearheads never miss and whose ale keeps the gods deathless.

candidates.name: Ériu
candidates.one_line: Sovereignty goddess who traded Ireland's name for a promise from the invading Milesians.

candidates.name: The Cailleach
candidates.one_line: Divine winter hag old beyond reckoning who dropped the mountains from her apron.

candidates.name: Donn
candidates.one_line: First of the dead, gathering all souls to his storm-wracked house on the sea.

candidates.name: Rhiannon
candidates.one_line: Otherworld horse-lady no rider can catch, framed for eating her own child and made to carry guests like a mount.

candidates.name: Arawn
candidates.one_line: Courteous king of the otherworld Annwn who swaps faces with a mortal for a year to settle a duel.

candidates.name: Bran the Blessed
candidates.one_line: Giant king who waded the sea to war and whose severed head kept feasting company for eighty years.

candidates.name: Gwydion
candidates.one_line: Magician-trickster who conjures horses from mushrooms, steals otherworld pigs, and builds a wife from flowers.

candidates.name: Ceridwen
candidates.one_line: Keeper of the cauldron of inspiration whose brew, stolen by a serving-boy, birthed the greatest of bards.

candidates.name: Cernunnos
candidates.one_line: Antlered lord of beasts seated cross-legged with torc and ram-horned serpent — one carved name, no surviving myth, immense modern afterlife.

candidates.name: Epona
candidates.one_line: Gaulish horse goddess so beloved the Roman cavalry adopted her wholesale into its own calendar.

candidates.name: Taranis
candidates.one_line: Wheel-bearing thunderer of Gaul whom Lucan names among the gods paid in human blood.

candidates.name: Toutatis
candidates.one_line: Tribal protector god of Gaul, sworn by in every Asterix album ('By Toutatis!').

candidates.name: Balor
candidates.one_line: Fomorian tyrant whose lidded eye kills armies when levered open — slain through it by his own grandson, as foretold.

candidates.name: Bres
candidates.one_line: Beautiful, stingy half-Fomorian king unmade by the first satire ever spoken, who bought his life with farming secrets.

candidates.name: Medb
candidates.one_line: Iron-willed queen of Connacht who launched a war over one brown bull to out-own her husband.

candidates.name: Cú Chulainn
candidates.one_line: Ulster's boy-warrior whose battle-warp turns him monstrous — held a whole army alone at the ford.

candidates.name: Fionn mac Cumhaill
candidates.one_line: Leader of the Fianna who tasted the Salmon of Knowledge and sucks his thumb for wisdom.

candidates.name: Diarmuid Ua Duibhne
candidates.one_line: Warrior with a love-spot no woman can resist, doomed to die gored by an enchanted boar.

candidates.name: Oisín
candidates.one_line: Warrior-poet who spent three centuries in the Land of Youth and crumbled to age the moment he touched home soil.

candidates.name: Lleu Llaw Gyffes
candidates.one_line: Cursed to have no name, no arms, no wife — tricked into all three, then unmade into a rotting eagle and sung back to manhood.

candidates.name: The Fomorians
candidates.one_line: Misshapen sea-race from beyond the waves who tax and terrorize every people that settles Ireland.

candidates.name: Dullahan
candidates.one_line: Headless rider with a spine-whip who speaks a name once — and that person dies.

candidates.name: Twrch Trwyth
candidates.one_line: King cursed into a colossal boar, hunted across two countries for the comb and shears between his ears.

candidates.name: Cŵn Annwn
candidates.one_line: White, red-eared hounds of the otherworld whose cry in the night sky heralds a death.

candidates.name: Y Ddraig Goch
candidates.one_line: The red dragon that battles the white beneath Britain — and still flies on the Welsh flag.

candidates.name: Leprechaun
candidates.one_line: Solitary fairy cobbler hoarding gold at the rainbow's end — catch him and he'll cheat you out of the wish.

candidates.name: Blodeuwedd
candidates.one_line: Wife conjured from oak, broom, and meadowsweet who plotted her husband's death and was remade as an owl.

candidates.name: Banshee
candidates.one_line: Keening woman of the mounds whose wail outside the window means a death in the family.

candidates.name: Púca
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting trickster — black horse, goat, hare — who offers wild rides and sours the unpicked harvest.

candidates.name: Lia Fáil
candidates.one_line: The Stone of Destiny at Tara that screams aloud under the foot of the rightful king.

candidates.name: Gáe Bulg
candidates.one_line: Cú Chulainn's barbed spear cast from the fork of the foot — it opens into thirty barbs inside a body and cannot be withdrawn.

candidates.name: Cauldron of the Dagda
candidates.one_line: One of the Four Treasures of the Tuatha Dé Danann — no company ever leaves it unsatisfied.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in the Celtic family is culturally closed or restricted — all material is fully published, public-sphere, and freely depicted by the living revival communities themselves. D-4 exclusions (real or plausibly historical humans, story-only material): Taliesin — a historical 6th-century Brittonic bard onto whom the Ceridwen/shape-shifting legend cycle accreted; his legend is usable as story material but he is excluded as an entity. Boudica and Vercingetorix — historical figures sometimes mythologized in modern media, excluded. St. Brigid of Kildare — historical saint (living major-religion veneration, feast now an Irish public holiday); excluded as an entity, but her syncretism with the goddess Brigid is flagged on that candidate. Kings of the Irish Cycle of the Kings (Cormac mac Airt, Conn Cétchathach, Niall Noígíallach, etc.) — euhemerized semi-historical dynasts; their cycles are rich story-only material but they are excluded as entities pending owner review. Arthurian matter (Arthur, Merlin/Myrddin, Excalibur) is deliberately left to a separate Arthurian/Brythonic-literary tradition census: Arthur carries claimed historicity and the matter is dominated by later medieval romance rather than Celtic myth proper — only genuinely mythological Welsh figures adjacent to it (Twrch Trwyth from Culhwch and Olwen) are listed here.

candidates.name: Jade Emperor (Yuhuang Dadi)
candidates.one_line: Supreme bureaucrat of Heaven who audits every god's performance and promotes or demotes them like a celestial HR department.

candidates.name: Sanqing (Three Pure Ones)
candidates.one_line: The Taoist trinity above even the Jade Emperor — pure emanations of the Dao itself; note: the Taishang Laojun aspect is entangled with the possibly-historical Laozi (borderline call, mythological cosmic persona included, historical man excluded).

candidates.name: Xiwangmu (Queen Mother of the West)
candidates.one_line: Tiger-toothed sovereign of Kunlun who hosts the immortals' banquet and controls the peaches of eternal life.

candidates.name: Caishen (God of Wealth)
candidates.one_line: The god everyone in the casino already prays to — money-luck incarnate, greeted with firecrackers on the fifth day of New Year; note: some regional Caishen identifications (e.g. Bi Gan, Zhao Gongming as historicized) are borderline, the composite wealth-god persona is mythological.

candidates.name: Fu Lu Shou (Three Star Gods)
candidates.one_line: Luck, Prosperity, and Longevity as a matched set of smiling star-gods on every shop shelf — casino nobility by portfolio.

candidates.name: Nüwa
candidates.one_line: Snake-bodied creatrix who molded humanity from clay and patched the broken sky with five-colored stones.

candidates.name: Pangu
candidates.one_line: The primordial giant who split heaven from earth and whose dying body became the world — a god who literally spent himself to zero.

candidates.name: Huangdi (Yellow Emperor)
candidates.one_line: Legendary divine sovereign and ancestor-god of the Chinese people, war-winner over Chiyou; borderline call: treated as mythological culture-god (no historical verification), still receives state-level veneration.

candidates.name: Yanluo Wang (King Yama)
candidates.one_line: Magistrate-king of the underworld courts who sentences souls by ledger — death run as a bureaucracy.

candidates.name: Zao Jun (Kitchen God)
candidates.one_line: The household informant who files an annual report on every family to Heaven — bribed with sticky candy so he can only mumble sweet things.

candidates.name: Tudi Gong (Earth God)
candidates.one_line: The humble neighborhood god of land and small fortunes — the deity Taiwanese lottery players petition first.

candidates.name: Zhenwu (Xuanwu, the Dark Warrior)
candidates.one_line: Barefoot martial god of the north who subdued his own extracted guts (turned turtle-and-snake demons) and rules from Wudang mountain.

candidates.name: Erlang Shen
candidates.one_line: Three-eyed warrior god with a truth-seeing brow-eye and a celestial hound, the only fighter to ever match Sun Wukong; note: partial roots in historicized figures (Li Bing's son), the three-eyed persona is fully mythological.

candidates.name: Nezha
candidates.one_line: Lotus-reborn boy god on wind-fire wheels who flayed his own flesh to repay his parents and now headlines China's biggest animated films.

candidates.name: Ao Guang (Dragon King of the East Sea)
candidates.one_line: Sea-sovereign of rain and storms, perpetually humiliated by heroes — a proud god forever one grievance from ruin.

candidates.name: Chang'e
candidates.one_line: The moon goddess who drank the stolen elixir and floated into eternal lunar exile — namesake of China's real lunar program.

candidates.name: Zhong Kui
candidates.one_line: Demon-queller with the ugliest face in Heaven — a wronged scholar-ghost who died in protest and was posthumously hired to eat ghosts; note: his 'failed examinee' backstory is legend, not a verified historical person.

candidates.name: Gonggong
candidates.one_line: The water god who lost the war for heaven and headbutted the pillar of the sky in despair, tilting the world forever.

candidates.name: Xingtian
candidates.one_line: Beheaded by the Yellow Emperor, he grew eyes in his nipples and a mouth in his navel and kept fighting — the patron saint of nothing-left-to-lose.

candidates.name: Sun Wukong (Monkey King)
candidates.one_line: Stone-born trickster who ate the peaches of immortality, erased his name from Death's ledger, and warred on Heaven itself — global icon from Dragon Ball to Black Myth.

candidates.name: Zhu Bajie (Pigsy)
candidates.one_line: Fallen marshal of Heaven's navy reborn as a gluttonous pig-man — a demoted immortal gambling on redemption.

candidates.name: Houyi
candidates.one_line: The archer who shot down nine of ten suns to save the world, then lost his elixir, his wife, and his immortality.

candidates.name: Yu the Great
candidates.one_line: Flood-tamer who reshaped rivers for thirteen years without once going home; borderline call: legendary sovereign with no historical verification, treated as mythological (later dynastic claims noted).

candidates.name: Hua Mulan
candidates.one_line: The daughter who rode to war in her father's place for twelve years undetected — a ballad heroine, not a verified historical person, now a global name.

candidates.name: Baigujing (White Bone Demon)
candidates.one_line: A skeleton-spirit who wears three different innocent human disguises in a single day to reach the flesh of the immortal-to-be.

candidates.name: Niu Mowang (Bull Demon King)
candidates.one_line: The Monkey King's sworn brother turned arch-enemy — a shape-shifting demon warlord with a family grudge.

candidates.name: Niutou & Mamian (Ox-Head and Horse-Face)
candidates.one_line: The underworld's inseparable arrest team — beast-headed bailiffs who drag fresh souls to court.

candidates.name: Jiangshi
candidates.one_line: The stiff-limbed hopping corpse that drains life-breath, repelled by talisman paper on the forehead — a whole horror-comedy genre in one monster.

candidates.name: Qilin
candidates.one_line: The gentle chimerical unicorn-of-the-East whose appearance announces the birth or death of a sage.

candidates.name: Fenghuang
candidates.one_line: The Chinese phoenix, sovereign of all birds and emblem of the empress — harmony given plumage.

candidates.name: Pixiu
candidates.one_line: Winged lion that devours gold and has no anus — wealth goes in and never comes out, which is why gamblers carry one.

candidates.name: Nian
candidates.one_line: The New Year monster scared off by red paper and firecrackers — the reason the holiday looks the way it does.

candidates.name: Bai Suzhen (Madam White Snake)
candidates.one_line: A thousand-year snake spirit who married a mortal for love and was entombed under a pagoda for it — China's great doomed-romance legend.

candidates.name: Zhinü & Niulang (Weaver Girl and Cowherd)
candidates.one_line: Star-crossed lovers separated by the Milky Way, reunited one night a year across a bridge of magpies — the origin of Qixi, China's Valentine's Day.

candidates.name: Meng Po
candidates.one_line: The old lady at the exit of the underworld whose soup erases every memory before reincarnation — the house always clears the table.

candidates.name: Hei Bai Wuchang (Black and White Impermanence)
candidates.one_line: The tall-hatted double act of death — one drowned, one hanged — who escort souls and, in Taiwan, are petitioned for lottery numbers.

candidates.name: Huli Jing (Nine-Tailed Fox Spirit)
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting fox seductress whose nine-tailed form has conquered global games and anime; the dynasty-toppling Daji incarnation is Fengshen material.

candidates.name: Jingwei
candidates.one_line: A drowned girl reborn as a small bird who drops pebbles into the sea forever, determined to fill the ocean that killed her.

candidates.name: Ruyi Jingu Bang
candidates.one_line: Sun Wukong's wish-fulfilling iron staff — an ocean-pillar that shrinks to a needle behind his ear and weighs eight tons regardless.

candidates.name: Peaches of Immortality
candidates.one_line: Xiwangmu's orchard fruit that ripens once in millennia and confers eternal life — the most stolen item in Heaven.

closed_material_notes: D-4 EXCLUSIONS (deified historical humans — story-only material, not entities): Guan Yu / Guandi (historical Han general, now one of the most-worshipped gods in the Chinese world; his Three Kingdoms legend cycle and 'god of war and merchants/sworn brotherhood' cult are premium story-only material — note he is ALSO petitioned by gamblers and police alike); Mazu (Lin Moniang, 10th-c. Fujianese woman, sea goddess with tens of millions of devotees — her sea-rescue cycle is story-only); Laozi as a historical person (his cosmic emanation persona is folded into the Sanqing entry — borderline call flagged there); Zhang Daoling (founder of Celestial Masters Taoism); Ji Gong (historical Song monk); Wong Tai Sin (historical hermit, major Hong Kong luck cult — a real loss for the casino theme, story-only); Bao Zheng / Judge Bao and Yue Fei (historical officials deified in folk religion); Baosheng Dadi (historical physician). EIGHT IMMORTALS — split verdict per D-4: EXCLUDED as entities: Zhang Guolao (attested Tang-dynasty fangshi), Lü Dongbin (probable Tang historical figure per Song sources), Han Xiangzi (tied to the historical Han Yu's family), Cao Guojiu (tied to the historical Song Empress Cao's clan). Borderline-legendary members (Tieguai Li, He Xiangu, Lan Caihe, Zhongli Quan) have no solid historical verification and could be admitted in a later wave with owner review; I left the whole group out of the candidate list this wave to keep the boundary clean — the 'Eight Immortals crossing the sea, each showing their powers' cycle itself is excellent story-only material and a literal gambling-adjacent proverb. CLOSED/RESTRICTED MATERIAL: Chinese folk mythology is broadly public-sphere with no cultural closure comparable to initiatory traditions; the one genuine restriction is esoteric Taoist liturgy — talismanic registers (lu) and internal-alchemy transmission texts are initiation-transmitted within Taoist lineages and were NOT collected; the public mythological personas of the same deities are unrestricted. Modern IP note: the figures are public domain, but the current Nezha and White Snake animated film franchises and Disney's Mulan are protected modern IP — designs must not resemble them.

candidates.name: Ra
candidates.one_line: The sun itself at the head of the pantheon, sailing a solar barque through the underworld every single night to be reborn at dawn.

candidates.name: Anubis
candidates.one_line: Jackal-headed embalmer who weighs your heart against a feather and stands guard over every tomb.

candidates.name: Osiris
candidates.one_line: Murdered, dismembered into pieces, reassembled, and crowned king of the dead — the god who lost everything once already.

candidates.name: Isis
candidates.one_line: Master magician who conned Ra's secret name out of him and resurrected her murdered husband; her cult later swept the whole Roman world.

candidates.name: Horus
candidates.one_line: Falcon sky-god who fought an eighty-year grudge match against his uncle Set for the throne of Egypt.

candidates.name: Set
candidates.one_line: Storm-and-chaos god who murdered his brother, lost the throne, and was demonized until his own cult collapsed — yet still spears Apep from Ra's barque nightly.

candidates.name: Thoth
candidates.one_line: Ibis-headed scribe and referee of the gods who once WON a game of senet against the moon, gambling five extra days into the calendar.

candidates.name: Bastet
candidates.one_line: Cat goddess of home, festivity, and protection whose festivals were the rowdiest parties in the ancient world.

candidates.name: Sekhmet
candidates.one_line: Lioness of plague and slaughter who nearly annihilated humanity until the gods got her blackout drunk on red-dyed beer.

candidates.name: Hathor
candidates.one_line: Golden cow-horned lady of love, music, and drunkenness — the pleasant face that Sekhmet's rage wears on good days.

candidates.name: Sobek
candidates.one_line: Crocodile god of the Nile's raw power, fed honey cakes by priests who kept live jeweled crocodiles in temple pools.

candidates.name: Amun
candidates.one_line: 'The Hidden One' — an invisible air god who merged with Ra and became the richest, most powerful state deity Egypt ever had.

candidates.name: Ptah
candidates.one_line: Memphite craftsman-creator who spoke the world into existence with heart and tongue — patron of every maker.

candidates.name: Khonsu
candidates.one_line: Moon god who gambled his own moonlight at senet against Thoth and LOST — which is why the moon wanes; lately a pop-culture star via Moon Knight.

candidates.name: Ma'at
candidates.one_line: Truth, balance, and cosmic order personified as a goddess whose single ostrich feather outweighs a sinful heart.

candidates.name: Nephthys
candidates.one_line: Set's estranged wife who defected to mourn Osiris — mistress of the funeral lament, mother of Anubis.

candidates.name: Bes
candidates.one_line: Grimacing dwarf god of luck, childbirth, and the home who scares off demons by being uglier and louder than they are.

candidates.name: Taweret
candidates.one_line: Pregnant hippo goddess with crocodile back and lion paws, fierce protector of mothers — recently beloved from Moon Knight.

candidates.name: Nut
candidates.one_line: The sky is her star-spangled body, arched over the earth, swallowing the sun each night and birthing it each morning.

candidates.name: Atum
candidates.one_line: Self-created first god who prophesies he will one day unmake all creation back into the primordial flood, himself included.

candidates.name: Neith
candidates.one_line: Primordial weaver-warrior so senior the gods asked HER to arbitrate the Horus-vs-Set lawsuit.

candidates.name: Khnum
candidates.one_line: Ram-headed potter who throws every human being on his wheel from Nile clay before birth.

candidates.name: Wadjet
candidates.one_line: The rearing cobra on Pharaoh's brow, spitting fire at the crown's enemies.

candidates.name: Wepwawet
candidates.one_line: Wolf-standard 'Opener of the Ways' who scouts the road ahead for armies and for the dead.

candidates.name: Shai
candidates.one_line: Fate itself as a god — born beside you, allotting your lifespan and luck, and standing next to you at the final weighing.

candidates.name: Renenutet
candidates.one_line: Cobra goddess of harvest-fortune who whispers each newborn's secret true name — Shai's consort in the economy of luck.

candidates.name: Heka
candidates.one_line: Magic personified — the raw force the gods themselves must borrow to get anything done.

candidates.name: Serapis
candidates.one_line: Boardroom-merger deity: Osiris and Apis fused with a Greek face by the Ptolemies to give two peoples one god.

candidates.name: Apep (Apophis)
candidates.one_line: The world-ending chaos serpent who ambushes the sun's barque every night and is speared, hacked, and unmade daily — only to reform.

candidates.name: Ammit
candidates.one_line: Crocodile-lion-hippo 'Devourer of the Dead' waiting under the scales to eat hearts that fail the weighing — a second, final death.

candidates.name: Great Sphinx / sphinxes
candidates.one_line: Lion-bodied royal guardian colossus — the Egyptian original that lent its shape to every riddle-monster after it.

candidates.name: Bennu
candidates.one_line: Self-created heron of rebirth perched on the first mound of creation — the prototype the Greeks turned into the phoenix.

candidates.name: Medjed
candidates.one_line: 'The Smiter' — a nameless-faced Book of the Dead ghost drawn as a sheet with eyes and feet, now an unlikely internet and Japanese-games cult icon.

candidates.name: Bata
candidates.one_line: Wronged herdsman of the Tale of Two Brothers who hides his heart in a tree, dies and returns through serial transformations — bull, tree, prince.

candidates.name: Eye of Horus (wedjat)
candidates.one_line: Horus's torn-out eye, healed and made whole — Egypt's universal amulet of protection and restoration.

candidates.name: Ankh
candidates.one_line: The looped cross of life itself, pressed by gods to mortal lips to fill them with breath.

candidates.name: Book of Thoth
candidates.one_line: A spellbook locked in nested chests at the bottom of the Nile that grants god-sight and ruins everyone who steals it.

candidates.name: Scales of Ma'at
candidates.one_line: The great balance of the Hall of Two Truths, where a human heart is weighed against a single feather.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this tradition is culturally closed or ritually restricted — ancient Egyptian religion is fully published and the small Kemetic revival is open-practice, so no material was withheld on sensitivity grounds. D-4 exclusions (real historical humans, including deified ones, omitted from the candidate list but usable as story-only material): Imhotep (Third Dynasty architect-physician deified as a god of medicine; his mummy-monster movie persona is modern IP, not myth); Amenhotep son of Hapu (deified scribe-sage); deified pharaohs and royal cults generally (e.g. Ramesses II, the Ptolemaic ruler cults, Cleopatra VII); and Setne Khamwas, the legendary magician of the demotic Setne cycle, who is a fictionalized real prince (Khaemweset, son of Ramesses II) — his tales remain excellent story-only material and their central mythic object, the Book of Thoth, IS retained as an artifact candidate since the book itself is divine, not human.

candidates.name: Zeus
candidates.one_line: Sky-king of Olympus who won the cosmos in a three-way casting of lots with his brothers — thunderbolt in hand, debts of prophecy at his back.

candidates.name: Hera
candidates.one_line: Queen of the gods and patron of marriage, whose grudges outlast dynasties.

candidates.name: Poseidon
candidates.one_line: Earth-shaker who drew the sea by lot and never forgave losing Athens to Athena in a judged contest.

candidates.name: Hades
candidates.one_line: Lord of the dead who drew the short lot for the underworld — the house that always collects, eventually.

candidates.name: Athena
candidates.one_line: Grey-eyed strategist born from Zeus's skull; wins her wagers by out-thinking the table.

candidates.name: Apollo
candidates.one_line: God of prophecy, plague, and music whose oracle at Delphi set odds for the whole ancient world.

candidates.name: Artemis
candidates.one_line: Virgin huntress of the wild moon; her arrows never miss and her rules are non-negotiable.

candidates.name: Ares
candidates.one_line: Bloody-minded war god the other Olympians despise but keep betting on.

candidates.name: Aphrodite
candidates.one_line: Foam-born goddess of desire whose golden-apple verdict started the Trojan War.

candidates.name: Hermes
candidates.one_line: Winged messenger, patron of thieves and travelers, credited inventor of knucklebone dice who played the gods for favors — a windfall was literally a 'hermaion'.

candidates.name: Hephaestus
candidates.one_line: Lame smith-god flung from Olympus who forges the artifacts everyone else gambles for.

candidates.name: Demeter
candidates.one_line: Grain-mother who starved the world into renegotiating her daughter's underworld contract.

candidates.name: Dionysus
candidates.one_line: Twice-born god of wine, ecstasy, and theater — the house entertainer who can unmake a king's mind.

candidates.name: Persephone
candidates.one_line: Spring maiden bound to the underworld by six pomegranate seeds — queen of a seasonal double life.

candidates.name: Tyche
candidates.one_line: Goddess of chance itself, city-fortune personified — as Roman Fortuna she spins the original wheel of fortune.

candidates.name: Eros
candidates.one_line: Primal-turned-cherubic god of love whose arrows rig every romantic bet on the board.

candidates.name: Kronos
candidates.one_line: Titan king who ate his children to dodge a prophecy, lost anyway, and was cast into Tartarus — a deposed high-roller with nothing left to lose.

candidates.name: Pan
candidates.one_line: Goat-legged god of wild panic — the only Olympian-age god antiquity itself pronounced dead ('Great Pan is dead'), perfect for a comeback stake.

candidates.name: Janus
candidates.one_line: Two-faced Roman god of doorways, beginnings, and endings with no Greek counterpart — he sees both sides of every wager.

candidates.name: Hecate
candidates.one_line: Torch-bearing goddess of crossroads, witchcraft, and thresholds; notably retains active modern neopagan devotion.

candidates.name: Prometheus
candidates.one_line: Titan who gambled against Zeus with a rigged sacrifice and stolen fire, and paid with an eternity of eagles — the archetypal all-in loser who was right.

candidates.name: The Moirai (Fates)
candidates.one_line: Three spinners who measure and cut every thread of destiny — the odds-setters even Zeus defers to.

candidates.name: Heracles
candidates.one_line: Twelve-labor strongman who wrestled Death himself for a queen's life and won a seat on Olympus.

candidates.name: Odysseus
candidates.one_line: The trickster-general of the Trojan Horse who bet ten years of his life against gods and monsters to get home.

candidates.name: Achilles
candidates.one_line: Near-invulnerable warrior who chose a short glorious life over a long forgotten one — the ultimate high-stakes trade.

candidates.name: Perseus
candidates.one_line: Gorgon-slayer flying on borrowed divine gear — proof the house sometimes staked a mortal.

candidates.name: Orpheus
candidates.one_line: Musician who charmed the underworld into a one-condition wager for his wife — and looked back.

candidates.name: Medusa
candidates.one_line: Snake-haired Gorgon whose gaze turns contestants to statuary — a boss with a mirror-based win condition built in.

candidates.name: Minotaur
candidates.one_line: Bull-headed prisoner of the Labyrinth, fed on tribute — the original dungeon boss in the original dungeon.

candidates.name: Cerberus
candidates.one_line: Three-headed hound guarding the exit of the dead; bribable with honeyed cakes and music.

candidates.name: Lernaean Hydra
candidates.one_line: Many-headed swamp serpent that grows two heads per decapitation — a mechanic, not a monster.

candidates.name: Pegasus
candidates.one_line: Winged horse sprung from Medusa's neck — mount, prize, and constellation.

candidates.name: Sphinx
candidates.one_line: Riddle-posing lion-woman who ran a death-stakes quiz show outside Thebes and self-destructed on her first loss.

candidates.name: Typhon
candidates.one_line: Hundred-headed storm-giant who nearly unseated Zeus — the monster the whole pantheon fled from.

candidates.name: Charon
candidates.one_line: The underworld ferryman who takes exactly one coin — the afterlife's toll booth and the cheapest bet in mythology.

candidates.name: The Erinyes (Furies)
candidates.one_line: Snake-wreathed avengers of blood-guilt who collect debts no court can cancel.

candidates.name: Sisyphus
candidates.one_line: The king who cheated Death twice and now rolls the boulder forever — the eternal gambler still playing a rigged table.

candidates.name: Midas
candidates.one_line: The king whose golden touch turned wealth into a curse — a casino parable in one wish.

candidates.name: Golden Fleece
candidates.one_line: Dragon-guarded ram's fleece worth crewing a ship of heroes to steal — the prototype quest MacGuffin.

candidates.name: Pandora's Pithos (Pandora's Box)
candidates.one_line: The jar that loosed every evil into the world and kept only Hope — the worst loot box ever opened.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this tradition is culturally closed or restricted — it is a fully public-sphere, public-domain corpus. D-4 exclusions applied: (1) Roman imperial-cult deified humans (Divus Julius/Julius Caesar, Divus Augustus, and later divi) are real historical people and are omitted as entities; their apotheosis legends are story-only material. (2) Romulus/Quirinus omitted as a quasi-historical founder-cult figure straddling the D-4 line; his founding legend is usable story material. (3) Alexander the Great's extensive legend cycle (divine sonship of Zeus-Ammon) is excluded as a historical human; story-only. (4) Deified mortals of the mystery/heroic borderland (e.g. Asclepius — mythic but sometimes claimed historical) were left off this wave's list conservatively; Asclepius is defensible as fully mythic if the owner wants him later. Caveat on an included entry: the folk figure Midas shares a name with a historical Phrygian king (Mita of Mushki); only the purely legendary golden-touch/ass's-ears cycle is intended, and the owner may re-adjudicate under D-4. Depth note: this family has far more legitimate candidates than the 40-cap allows (Hestia, Nike, Nemesis, Helios, Selene, Iris [messenger], Ouranos [forsaken], Thanatos, Theseus, Jason, Atalanta, Aeneas, Chimera, Sirens, Scylla/Charybdis, Chiron, Muses, nymphs/satyrs, Trojan Horse, Aegis, Caduceus, Cornucopia [fortune-attribute item]) — Wave 2 extraction will not run dry; source_quality 5 supports deep extraction.

candidates.name: Shiva
candidates.one_line: The cosmic destroyer-ascetic who lost everything — down to his clothes — at dice to his own wife, then decreed the game to mankind.

candidates.name: Vishnu
candidates.one_line: The serene preserver who wagers on the world's survival across ten descending avatars.

candidates.name: Krishna
candidates.one_line: The blue-skinned trickster-charioteer who counsels heroes at the table of war and never shows his whole hand.

candidates.name: Rama
candidates.one_line: The exiled prince-avatar of perfect dharma whose fourteen-year losing streak ends in the burning of Lanka.

candidates.name: Ganesha
candidates.one_line: The elephant-headed remover of obstacles, invoked before every venture — the house's favorite opening bet.

candidates.name: Lakshmi
candidates.one_line: The lotus-borne goddess of wealth and fortune who stands invisibly behind the winning player on Diwali night.

candidates.name: Durga
candidates.one_line: The ten-armed war goddess assembled from the pooled powers of every god to break an unbeatable demon.

candidates.name: Kali
candidates.one_line: The black mother of time who dances on her own husband's chest wearing a garland of severed heads.

candidates.name: Hanuman
candidates.one_line: The immortal monkey devotee who leaped an ocean and once tried to swallow the sun as a fruit.

candidates.name: Parvati
candidates.one_line: The mountain goddess who beat the god of destruction at dice with Lakshmi quietly stacking her odds.

candidates.name: Saraswati
candidates.one_line: The white-robed goddess of knowledge, music, and speech, riding a swan through the currents of thought.

candidates.name: Brahma
candidates.one_line: The four-faced creator cursed to near-worshiplessness — a founder of the casino who can barely afford a table.

candidates.name: Indra
candidates.one_line: The thunderbolt-wielding king of heaven, once the Vedas' richest god, now a nervous minor shareholder.

candidates.name: Yama
candidates.one_line: The buffalo-riding lord of death with a noose and a ledger, famously argued into a refund by a mortal widow.

candidates.name: Kartikeya (Murugan)
candidates.one_line: The six-faced spear-bearing war god, commander of heaven's armies and beloved sovereign of the Tamil south.

candidates.name: Shani
candidates.one_line: The slow, iron-eyed planet-god Saturn whose gaze alone bankrupts kings — fortune's grim auditor.

candidates.name: Kubera
candidates.one_line: The pot-bellied treasurer of the gods, guardian of all buried wealth — the casino's natural banker.

candidates.name: Narada
candidates.one_line: The veena-strumming celestial sage who carries news between all three worlds and starts half the trouble in them.

candidates.name: Alakshmi
candidates.one_line: Misfortune personified, Lakshmi's inauspicious elder sister, ritually shooed from the house before every festival.

candidates.name: Arjuna
candidates.one_line: The peerless archer who received the Bhagavad Gita mid-battlefield, paralyzed between duty and grief.

candidates.name: Yudhishthira
candidates.one_line: The king of dharma who gambled away his kingdom, brothers, wife, and self in a single rigged dice match.

candidates.name: Nala
candidates.one_line: A king possessed by the dice-demon who diced away his realm, then won it back with the secret of the dice.

candidates.name: Draupadi
candidates.one_line: The fire-born queen staked and lost at dice, whose humiliation guaranteed a war that emptied the earth.

candidates.name: Savitri
candidates.one_line: The princess who followed Death himself down the road and out-argued him into returning her husband's soul.

candidates.name: Ravana
candidates.one_line: The ten-headed scholar-king of Lanka who could lift a mountain, whose one theft cost him everything.

candidates.name: Shakuni
candidates.one_line: The vengeful uncle whose loaded dice — carved, some say, from his father's bones — never once rolled false for him.

candidates.name: Mahishasura
candidates.one_line: The shape-shifting buffalo demon who could be slain by no man or god, only by a goddess with ten arms.

candidates.name: Rahu
candidates.one_line: A decapitated demon head, immortal from stolen nectar, who swallows the sun and moon out of spite — eclipse as grudge.

candidates.name: Kali (dice-demon)
candidates.one_line: The spirit of the losing age itself, who lives inside dice and possessed King Nala to ruin him throw by throw.

candidates.name: Garuda
candidates.one_line: The sun-bright eagle-man who robbed heaven of its nectar to free his mother, now Vishnu's mount and the serpents' terror.

candidates.name: Shesha (Ananta)
candidates.one_line: The endless thousand-hooded world-serpent on whose coils Vishnu sleeps between universes.

candidates.name: Nandi
candidates.one_line: Shiva's white bull, gatekeeper of Kailasa, into whose ear worshippers whisper wishes for relay to the god.

candidates.name: Kamadhenu
candidates.one_line: The wish-granting mother of all cows, worth more than any kingdom and the cause of several wars over her ownership.

candidates.name: Apsara (Menaka/Urvashi)
candidates.one_line: Celestial dancers dispatched by nervous gods to seduce ascetics whose accumulating power threatens heaven's odds.

candidates.name: Vetala
candidates.one_line: A corpse-riding spirit who tells a king twenty-four riddle-tales, each with a life wagered on the answer.

candidates.name: Churel
candidates.one_line: The backwards-footed ghost of a wronged woman who drains the lifeblood of the men of the family that failed her.

candidates.name: Sudarshana Chakra
candidates.one_line: Vishnu's spinning discus of a hundred million spikes that decapitates at any distance and always returns.

candidates.name: Vajra
candidates.one_line: Indra's thunderbolt, forged from a sage's freely donated backbone — a weapon paid for in self-sacrifice.

candidates.name: Amrita
candidates.one_line: The nectar of immortality churned from a milk ocean, the highest-stakes pot gods and demons ever contested.

candidates.name: Syamantaka
candidates.one_line: A jewel that showers its righteous keeper in gold and destroys any unworthy holder — fortune with a fine-print curse.

closed_material_notes: D-4 exclusions (real historical humans, including actively deified ones, omitted as entities): Adi Shankara, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Mirabai, Sai Baba of Shirdi, Swaminarayan, and modern guru figures — several receive active temple worship but are historical people; their hagiographic legend cycles are story-only material. The Buddha (listed in some Puranas as a Vishnu avatar) is likewise a historical human and excluded. Nothing in the public epic/Puranic corpus is formally closed — it is among the most widely published mythologies on earth — but two honest caveats: (1) esoteric tantric practice material (initiatory rites, specific mantras) is traditionally guru-transmitted and restricted, and was not collected; (2) the entire pantheon is under active worship by ~1.2B people with documented sensitivity to commercial/disrespectful deity depiction, so every deity is flagged "living" and should default to the off-screen-investor presentation per the family guidance.

candidates.name: Amaterasu
candidates.one_line: Sun goddess and imperial ancestress who once hid the world's light in a cave and had to be lured out with a mirror and a bawdy dance.

candidates.name: Susanoo
candidates.one_line: Storm god exiled from heaven who redeemed himself by getting an eight-headed serpent blackout drunk and carving it apart.

candidates.name: Tsukuyomi
candidates.one_line: Moon god who killed the food goddess over a disgusting dinner and has been estranged from his sister the sun ever since — why day and night never meet.

candidates.name: Izanagi
candidates.one_line: Primordial father who stirred the sea into islands, chased his dead wife into the underworld, and birthed the sun, moon, and storm while washing off the filth of Yomi.

candidates.name: Izanami
candidates.one_line: Creator mother burned to death birthing fire, now a rotting queen of the dead who swore to strangle a thousand lives a day — a goddess who lost everything and rules from the bottom.

candidates.name: Inari
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting kami of rice, commerce, and worldly success with 30,000+ shrines and an army of white fox messengers — Japan's patron of getting rich.

candidates.name: Hachiman
candidates.one_line: God of archery and war, divine protector who wrecked two Mongol invasion fleets with kamikaze winds; syncretically identified with the legendary Emperor Ojin.

candidates.name: Okuninushi
candidates.one_line: Master of the visible land who built the world with a trickster ally, then was forced to hand it all over to heaven's line — the great dispossessed lord, now god of unseen matchmaking.

candidates.name: Ebisu
candidates.one_line: Laughing fisher god of merchants and windfall luck — the only one of the Seven Lucky Gods born on Japanese soil, and famously a bit deaf to prayers.

candidates.name: Benzaiten
candidates.one_line: Biwa-strumming goddess of eloquence, music, water, and money-luck — Saraswati gone native, whose shrines sit on islands where gamblers wash their coins.

candidates.name: Daikokuten
candidates.one_line: Grinning god of wealth standing on rice bales with a mallet that rains coins — the fearsome Mahakala mellowed into Japan's kitchen-fat fortune lord.

candidates.name: Bishamonten
candidates.one_line: Armored war-lucky god of the north bearing a pagoda of treasure — the fighter's patron among the Seven Lucky Gods.

candidates.name: Hotei
candidates.one_line: Pot-bellied wandering monk of contentment whose bottomless cloth sack holds gifts for the deserving — the West's 'Laughing Buddha' is this lucky god.

candidates.name: Fukurokuju
candidates.one_line: Tiny sage with a towering forehead who carries longevity, wealth, and happiness in one absurd silhouette.

candidates.name: Jurojin
candidates.one_line: Wine-loving god of long life who travels with a 1,500-year-old deer and is chronically confused with Fukurokuju — even luck gods have branding problems.

candidates.name: Ame-no-Uzume
candidates.one_line: Goddess of dawn and revelry whose scandalous cave-front striptease made the gods laugh so hard the sun came back — the original showstopper.

candidates.name: Sarutahiko
candidates.one_line: Towering long-nosed earthly kami who stood at the crossroads of heaven and earth to guide the divine grandson down — patron of guidance and martial ways.

candidates.name: Takemikazuchi
candidates.one_line: Thunder-sword god who won Japan for heaven in a grappling match on a beach — the mythic origin of sumo.

candidates.name: Raijin
candidates.one_line: Demon-faced thunder god hammering a ring of drums across the storm clouds — half of Japanese art's most famous double act.

candidates.name: Fujin
candidates.one_line: Wild-haired wind god carrying every gale in a sack slung over his shoulders, forever racing his brother the thunder.

candidates.name: Kagutsuchi
candidates.one_line: Fire god beheaded by his own father moments after his birth killed his mother — from his spilled blood sprang a generation of sword gods.

candidates.name: Ryujin
candidates.one_line: Dragon king of the sea who rules the tides from a coral palace with jewels that command the ocean's ebb and flow.

candidates.name: Yamato Takeru
candidates.one_line: Doomed warrior prince who conquered in women's clothing and with the grass-cutting sword, then died cursed by a mountain god and flew away as a white bird.

candidates.name: Momotaro
candidates.one_line: Peach-born boy who recruited a dog, a monkey, and a pheasant with millet dumplings and sacked the ogres' island fortress.

candidates.name: Oni
candidates.one_line: Horned, tiger-pelted ogres with iron clubs — the stock demonic muscle of Japanese hell and every festival's bean-pelted villain.

candidates.name: Shuten-doji
candidates.one_line: Demon king of Mount Oe who drank the blood of kidnapped nobles as wine until heroes got him drunk on poisoned sake and took his head — which kept fighting.

candidates.name: Tamamo-no-Mae
candidates.one_line: Nine-tailed fox in the guise of the empire's most beautiful courtesan, whose unmasking chase ended with her spite fused into a killing stone.

candidates.name: Yamata no Orochi
candidates.one_line: Eight-headed, eight-tailed serpent spanning eight valleys, slain drunk on eight vats of sake — with a legendary sword hidden in its tail.

candidates.name: Kitsune
candidates.one_line: Fox spirits that grow a tail per century of cunning — divine white messengers of Inari at the shrine, seductive tricksters everywhere else.

candidates.name: Bake-danuki
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting raccoon dogs who turn leaves into money and their own anatomy into drums, boats, and shop signs — chaos with a straw hat.

candidates.name: Yatagarasu
candidates.one_line: Three-legged crow sent from heaven to guide the first emperor through the mountains — now the emblem on Japan's national football shirt.

candidates.name: Kappa
candidates.one_line: River goblin with a water-filled dish for a skull — deadly to swimmers, obsessed with cucumbers, and defeated by a polite bow.

candidates.name: Tengu
candidates.one_line: Long-nosed winged mountain ascetics who kidnap the arrogant and secretly taught Japan's greatest swordsmen their art.

candidates.name: Urashima Taro
candidates.one_line: Fisherman who spent three days feasting in the sea king's palace, came home three centuries late, and aged to dust opening the box he was told not to open.

candidates.name: Yuki-onna
candidates.one_line: Snow woman of terrible beauty who freezes travelers with her breath — and sometimes spares one, on a promise he will inevitably break.

candidates.name: Amabie
candidates.one_line: Glowing fish-bodied prophet from the sea who promises that spreading its picture wards off plague — resurrected as Japan's 2020 pandemic mascot.

candidates.name: Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi
candidates.one_line: The grass-cutting sword pulled from the serpent's tail, one of the three Imperial Regalia — the mythic blade of Japanese fiction, never once shown in public.

candidates.name: Yata no Kagami
candidates.one_line: The eight-span mirror that lured the sun goddess from her cave, enshrined at Ise as the vessel of her presence.

candidates.name: Yasakani no Magatama
candidates.one_line: The curved jewel of the Imperial Regalia, hung on the sacred tree before Amaterasu's cave — sovereignty in a comma-shaped bead.

candidates.name: Uchide no Kozuchi
candidates.one_line: Daikokuten's magic mallet that showers gold with every swing and grew the one-inch samurai to full size — pure jackpot in wooden form.

closed_material_notes: D-4 exclusions (real historical humans deified as kami — story-only material, not entities): Tenjin (Sugawara no Michizane, 845-903, scholar deified to appease his vengeful ghost — his onryo legend cycle is superb story material); Toshogu / Tosho Daigongen (Tokugawa Ieyasu, deified 1616); Emperor Meiji (Meiji Shrine); the war dead of Yasukuni and Nogi/Togo shrines (ALSO politically sensitive — recommend never used even as story material); Abe no Seimei (real 10th-c. onmyoji with a huge legend cycle, incl. the fox-mother Kuzunoha tale); Minamoto no Yorimitsu/Raiko and his Four Heavenly Kings (real 10th-11th-c. warriors whose legend cycle supplies the Shuten-doji and tsuchigumo slayings — the MONSTERS are listed as entities, the human slayers are not); Sakata no Kintoki (the historical seed of Kintaro); Empress Jingu; En no Gyoja. Hachiman is listed as an entity because the Emperor Ojin identification attaches to a figure of legendary rather than established historicity — flagged for owner review. Formally closed material: none in the initiation-secret sense — Shinto's core texts are published — but the three physical Imperial Regalia are never displayed (even the emperor does not view them) and are bound to the living imperial institution; the artifact entries cover only their public mythic versions and carry flag 'restricted'. Ainu kamuy and Ryukyuan religion are distinct living traditions deliberately NOT harvested under this record.

candidates.name: Quetzalcoatl
candidates.one_line: The Feathered Serpent — wind, learning, and the priesthood; tricked into disgrace, he burned his palaces and sailed east on a raft of serpents vowing to return.

candidates.name: Tezcatlipoca
candidates.one_line: Smoking Mirror, the omnipresent sorcerer-king of night and fate, 'Enemy of Both Sides,' who ruined Quetzalcoatl with a mirror and a drink.

candidates.name: Huitzilopochtli
candidates.one_line: Hummingbird of the South — the Mexica war-sun born fully armed to slaughter his four hundred star-siblings on Coatepec.

candidates.name: Tlaloc
candidates.one_line: Goggle-eyed lord of rain and lightning whose mountain paradise Tlalocan receives the drowned and the lightning-struck.

candidates.name: Mictlantecuhtli
candidates.one_line: Skeletal Lord of Mictlan who set rigged trials for Quetzalcoatl when he came begging for the bones of the dead.

candidates.name: Mictecacihuatl
candidates.one_line: Lady of the Dead, co-ruler of Mictlan, keeper of the bones — popularly (and loosely) linked to modern Day of the Dead imagery.

candidates.name: Xipe Totec
candidates.one_line: Our Lord the Flayed One — spring renewal god who wears the skin of the sacrificed as the earth wears new growth.

candidates.name: Coatlicue
candidates.one_line: Serpent-Skirt, the devouring earth mother whose colossal statue still unsettles visitors to the Museo Nacional.

candidates.name: Xolotl
candidates.one_line: Dog-headed twin of Quetzalcoatl, evening-star psychopomp who wept, shapeshifted, and fled to dodge his own sacrifice — and was killed anyway.

candidates.name: Xochiquetzal
candidates.one_line: Flower-Quetzal, goddess of love, beauty, weaving, and courtesans, abducted from paradise by Tezcatlipoca.

candidates.name: Xochipilli
candidates.one_line: Flower Prince — ecstasy, art, feasting, and games; patron of pleasure whose famous statue sits entranced on a bed of psychoactive blooms.

candidates.name: Macuilxochitl
candidates.one_line: Five-Flower, the dedicated Aztec god of gambling and games of chance — patolli players invoked him with every throw of the beans.

candidates.name: Tonatiuh
candidates.one_line: The Fifth Sun, tongue-out face of the Calendar Stone, who demanded blood tribute just to keep moving across the sky.

candidates.name: Nanahuatzin
candidates.one_line: The pustuled, penniless god who — having nothing to lose — leapt into the bonfire at Teotihuacan when the proud god flinched, and became the Sun.

candidates.name: Paynal
candidates.one_line: The swift herald and deputy of Huitzilopochtli, impersonated by a running messenger in Mexica festival rites.

candidates.name: Ehecatl
candidates.one_line: The wind aspect of Quetzalcoatl with a beaked mask, whose round temples let the breath of night flow around them.

candidates.name: Cihuacoatl
candidates.one_line: Serpent Woman, warlike midwife goddess heard wailing in the night for her children — a root often cited for La Llorona.

candidates.name: Itzpapalotl
candidates.one_line: Obsidian Butterfly — skeletal warrior goddess of the star demons who rules the paradise of Tamoanchan.

candidates.name: Kukulkan
candidates.one_line: The Maya Feathered Serpent (K'iche' Gucumatz), whose shadow still slithers down El Castillo at Chichén Itzá each equinox.

candidates.name: Itzamna
candidates.one_line: Aged supreme creator-sage of the Maya, inventor of writing and the calendar, lord of the sky.

candidates.name: Ixchel
candidates.one_line: Rainbow jaguar-goddess of the moon, midwifery, and weaving, whose Cozumel shrine drew canoe pilgrimages.

candidates.name: Chaac
candidates.one_line: Axe-wielding Maya rain god — still petitioned by name in Yucatec Ch'a Cháak field ceremonies today.

candidates.name: Huracan
candidates.one_line: Heart of Sky, one-legged storm creator of the Popol Vuh who spoke the world into being — and gave English the word 'hurricane'; still named in daykeeper prayers.

candidates.name: Ah Puch
candidates.one_line: Putrescent Maya death lord (God A / Kisin), belled and skeletal, stalking the ninth underworld layer.

candidates.name: Ixtab
candidates.one_line: Rope Woman of Landa's account, receiver of those who die by the noose — modern scholarship doubts the 'suicide goddess' framing; handle with care.

candidates.name: Hunahpu and Xbalanque (the Hero Twins)
candidates.one_line: Blowgunner twins summoned to Xibalba to play a ballgame with their lives as the stake — they lost, died, returned as tricksters, and unmade the lords of death before rising as sun and moon.

candidates.name: One Death and Seven Death (Lords of Xibalba)
candidates.one_line: Chief lords of the Maya underworld who lure the living into rigged ballgame wagers — and who staked everything against the Twins and were annihilated.

candidates.name: Tzitzimime
candidates.one_line: Skeletal star demons who plunge from the darkened sky to devour humanity if the sun ever fails at eclipse or era's end.

candidates.name: Vucub-Caquix (Seven Macaw)
candidates.one_line: The vainglorious false sun of the Popol Vuh, blowgunned off his perch and stripped of his jeweled teeth and metal eyes by the Twins.

candidates.name: Zipacna
candidates.one_line: Mountain-making crocodilian braggart, son of Seven Macaw, buried alive under the mountain he was tricked into carrying.

candidates.name: Cabrakan
candidates.one_line: The Earthquake, mountain-toppling brother of Zipacna, felled by a poisoned roast bird.

candidates.name: Camazotz
candidates.one_line: The killer bat of Xibalba's Bat House, who snatched off Hunahpu's head to be used as the ballgame ball.

candidates.name: Cipactli
candidates.one_line: The primordial crocodile-fish who bit off Tezcatlipoca's foot as bait and whose torn body became the earth itself.

candidates.name: Ahuitzotl
candidates.one_line: Spiny lake-monster with a clawed hand on its tail that drags fishermen under and eats only eyes, teeth, and nails.

candidates.name: Cihuateteo
candidates.one_line: Divinized ghosts of women who died in childbirth, haunting crossroads at dusk to seize children and madden travelers.

candidates.name: Alux
candidates.one_line: Knee-high Yucatec guardian imps of field and cenote — still propitiated with offerings and small shrine-houses by Maya farmers today.

candidates.name: Xtabay
candidates.one_line: Beautiful demon-woman of Yucatec folklore who waits beneath the ceiba to seduce and destroy men on night roads.

candidates.name: Maximón (San Simón)
candidates.one_line: Cigar-smoking, liquor-drinking trickster folk deity of highland Guatemala, actively venerated in cofradía shrines — owner review required.

candidates.name: Xiuhcoatl
