candidates.one_line: The Turquoise Fire-Serpent — the living lightning-weapon Huitzilopochtli used to dismember Coyolxauhqui at his birth.

candidates.name: Smoking Mirror of Tezcatlipoca
candidates.one_line: The scrying obsidian mirror (worn where a torn-off foot should be) in which the god watches every deed on earth.

closed_material_notes: D-4 exclusions (real historical humans, story-only material): Topiltzin Ce Acatl Quetzalcoatl (the historical Toltec priest-king whose legend fused with the god Quetzalcoatl — the god is listed, the man is not); Tecun Uman (K'iche' war leader vs. Alvarado, national hero with a quetzal legend cycle); Moctezuma II and the return-of-Quetzalcoatl legend complex; deified royal ancestors on Maya stelae (e.g., Pakal of Palenque, subject of modern 'ancient astronaut' pop myth). CLOSED/RESTRICTED material, reported not collected: (1) ajq'ij (daykeeper) tzolk'in divination protocols and day-lord ritual knowledge — initiatory, transmitted within K'iche'/Kaqchikel lineages; only the publicly published Popol Vuh layer is used. (2) The Talking Cross religion of the Cruzo'ob Maya (Quintana Roo) — an active shrine religion with guarded sanctuaries; excluded entirely. (3) Maximón/San Simón shrine practice is controlled by Santiago Atitlán cofradías; the widely published figure is listed with flags living+restricted for owner gating, but specific ritual detail is out of scope. (4) Santa Muerte is excluded as a contemporary religious movement whose claimed Aztec continuity (via Mictecacihuatl) is scholarly contested — Mictecacihuatl herself is listed on pre-contact grounds only. Note also that specific living Maya communities' patron-saint/mountain-spirit (dueño/witz) cults vary village by village and are treated as local living religion, not census material.

candidates.name: Inanna / Ishtar
candidates.one_line: Queen of Heaven — love, war, and ambition in one body; stormed the underworld and was hung dead on a hook before clawing her way back.

candidates.name: Enki / Ea
candidates.one_line: Trickster lord of the sweet deep, keeper of the me and maker of humanity — lost the divine decrees to Inanna in a drinking bout and cheerfully rigged the flood.

candidates.name: Marduk
candidates.one_line: Babylon's storm-champion who slew Tiamat and built the world from her corpse, taking kingship of the gods as his fee.

candidates.name: Tiamat
candidates.one_line: Primordial saltwater mother turned monster-queen — unmade in single combat, her split body became sky and earth.

candidates.name: Ereshkigal
candidates.one_line: Iron queen of the land of no return, who hung her own sister on a hook and won a husband by holding Nergal hostage with desire.

candidates.name: Nergal
candidates.one_line: God of plague and war who stormed the underworld with fourteen demons and ended up its king by marriage.

candidates.name: Anu
candidates.one_line: Remote sky-father and highest authority of the pantheon — the office every usurper myth is really about.

candidates.name: Enlil
candidates.one_line: Lord Wind, executive king of the gods who decreed the flood and was later quietly written out of his own throne by Marduk's priests.

candidates.name: Utu / Shamash
candidates.one_line: All-seeing sun and supreme judge — patron of travelers, oaths, and divination by extispicy.

candidates.name: Nanna / Sin
candidates.one_line: Moon god of Ur and Harran, cosmic timekeeper and father of the sun and Inanna.

candidates.name: Ninhursag / Ninmah
candidates.one_line: Mother of the mountain who shaped humans from clay — and lost a drunken creature-making contest to Enki.

candidates.name: Dumuzid / Tammuz
candidates.one_line: Shepherd-king consort handed to the underworld as Inanna's substitute — dies and returns every half-year.

candidates.name: Geshtinanna
candidates.one_line: Dumuzid's loyal sister who split his death sentence, spending half of every year below in his place.

candidates.name: Nabu
candidates.one_line: Divine scribe of Borsippa who writes the fates of all on his tablet each new year.

candidates.name: Ashur
candidates.one_line: Winged national god of Assyria who absorbed Enlil's and Marduk's myths wholesale as his empire grew.

candidates.name: Ninurta
candidates.one_line: Thunderbird-slaying warrior-farmer god who recovered the stolen Tablet of Destinies and named his trophy weapons one by one.

candidates.name: Adad / Ishkur
candidates.one_line: Storm god of the life-giving and city-drowning rain, oracle-lord alongside Shamash.

candidates.name: Apsu
candidates.one_line: Primordial freshwater father lulled to sleep and slain by Enki — the first god ever unmade, his body now the cosmic groundwater.

candidates.name: Erra
candidates.one_line: Restless plague-god who talked Marduk off his own throne and nearly burned Babylon for the thrill of it — the pantheon's all-in gambler temperament.

candidates.name: Namtar
candidates.one_line: Fate personified — Ereshkigal's herald and the underworld's process-server, who delivers each mortal's allotted death.

candidates.name: Ninshubur
candidates.one_line: Inanna's unshakeable vizier-herald who ran to three gods in turn to bargain her mistress back from death.

candidates.name: Qingu
candidates.one_line: Tiamat's upjumped general, briefly holder of the Tablet of Destinies — executed and drained so his blood could make humankind.

candidates.name: Gilgamesh
candidates.one_line: Two-thirds-divine tyrant of Uruk who fought a wild man into brotherhood, then chased immortality to the edge of the world and came home empty-handed and wise.

candidates.name: Enkidu
candidates.one_line: Wild man made from clay to be a king's equal — civilized by love, doomed by the gods' invoice for the Bull of Heaven.

candidates.name: Atrahasis / Utnapishtim / Ziusudra
candidates.one_line: The flood survivor — tipped off through a reed wall by Enki, granted the immortality Gilgamesh could not win.

candidates.name: Adapa
candidates.one_line: First of the sages, who broke the south wind's wing and then refused the bread of eternal life on bad advice from Enki.

candidates.name: Humbaba
candidates.one_line: Coiled-face guardian of the Cedar Forest with seven auras of terror — begged for his life and was beheaded anyway.

candidates.name: Bull of Heaven (Gugalanna)
candidates.one_line: Drought made flesh, loaned to a scorned Inanna to trample Uruk — its haunch hurled in the goddess's face.

candidates.name: Anzu
candidates.one_line: Lion-headed thunderbird that stole the Tablet of Destinies from Enlil's bath — an all-in heist that ended with its wings clipped by Ninurta.

candidates.name: Mushussu
candidates.one_line: The serpent-dragon of the Ishtar Gate — scaled body, lion forelegs, eagle talons, Marduk's leashed emblem.

candidates.name: Girtablullu (scorpion-men)
candidates.one_line: Colossal scorpion-bodied wardens of the mountain gate where the sun passes — their glance is death, yet they waved Gilgamesh through.

candidates.name: Pazuzu
candidates.one_line: Canine-faced king of the wind demons — so feared he was worn as an amulet to scare off worse demons than himself.

candidates.name: Lamashtu
candidates.one_line: Lion-headed daughter of Anu who slips into birthing rooms to snatch infants — the demoness even Pazuzu is invoked against.

candidates.name: Galla
candidates.one_line: Pitiless underworld constables who accept no bribe, drink no offering — they dragged Dumuzid down by the arms.

candidates.name: Lamassu / Shedu
candidates.one_line: Human-headed winged bull-colossi that flank palace gates — protective spirits of threshold and king.

candidates.name: Lilitu
candidates.one_line: Restless wind-maiden demon nesting in Inanna's huluppu tree — one debated root of the later Lilith figure.

candidates.name: Apkallu
candidates.one_line: Seven fish-cloaked antediluvian sages sent by Enki to teach humanity the arts of civilization.

candidates.name: Tablet of Destinies
candidates.one_line: The clay deed to the universe — whoever wears it decrees fate itself, which is why everyone from Anzu to Qingu tries to steal it.

candidates.name: The Me
candidates.one_line: The divine decrees of civilization — kingship, crafts, truth, deceit — carried off by boat after Inanna drank Enki under the table.

candidates.name: Plant of Heartbeat
candidates.one_line: The thorny rejuvenation herb Gilgamesh dove for and won — then lost to a passing snake while he bathed.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this tradition is culturally closed or restricted — the corpus is fully published, excavated, and in the public scholarly sphere. D-4 exclusions and borderline calls: (1) Deified historical kings excluded as entities — Sargon of Akkad and Naram-Sin (rich legend cycles: 'Sargon Birth Legend', 'Cuthean Legend') and Shulgi of Ur (deified in life, hymn corpus) are real humans; their legend cycles are available as story-only material. (2) Borderline Sumerian King List figures: Gilgamesh is INCLUDED — a historical kernel is possible but unproven, and the epic figure (two-thirds god) is treated by scholarship as a mythic-literary character; Etana (the eagle-flight king) and Lugalbanda (Gilgamesh's deified father) sit closer to the deified-king line and are OMITTED from the candidate list — both legend cycles (ETCSL, public domain translations) flagged as story-only material for owner review. (3) Lilith proper belongs to living Jewish tradition and is deferred to that census; only the Mesopotamian lilitu wind-spirit is listed here with a syncretic flag. (4) Modern-IP caution, not closure: D&D's five-headed Tiamat and The Exorcist's Pazuzu are protected modern depictions — the ancient figures and texts are public domain, so design must derive from primary sources, hence the ip:modern_ip_flagged flags.

candidates.name: Odin (Wotan/Woden)
candidates.one_line: One-eyed Allfather who pawned an eye for wisdom, hanged himself on the world-tree for the runes, and once wagered his head in a riddle-contest with a giant — a god who bets everything, always.

candidates.name: Thor (Donar/Thunor)
candidates.one_line: Hammer-swinging thunder god and giant-slayer, humiliated in Utgarda-Loki's rigged drinking contest and doomed to nine dying steps after killing the World Serpent.

candidates.name: Loki
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting trickster who literally wagered his own head against dwarf smiths — and welched on the neck — before engineering Baldr's death and his own torturous unmaking.

candidates.name: Freyja
candidates.one_line: Vanir goddess of love, seiðr-magic, and war who takes half the battle-slain and drives a cat-drawn chariot; the most-invoked goddess of the revival.

candidates.name: Frigg
candidates.one_line: Queen of Asgard who knows every fate but tells none, and who extracted oaths from all creation to shield Baldr — missing only the mistletoe.

candidates.name: Baldr
candidates.one_line: The shining god already slain by a blind brother's mistletoe dart — the pantheon's proof that gods can be unmade, waiting in Hel until after Ragnarök.

candidates.name: Tyr
candidates.one_line: One-handed god of law and war who fed his sword-hand to Fenrir as collateral on a bet the gods never meant to honor.

candidates.name: Heimdall
candidates.one_line: Nine-mothered watchman of the rainbow bridge who hears grass grow, fated to kill Loki and die of it in the same hour.

candidates.name: Freyr
candidates.one_line: God of harvest, peace, and prosperity who traded away his self-fighting sword for love — and will face Surtr barehanded because of it.

candidates.name: Njörðr
candidates.one_line: Vanir god of the sea, seafaring, and wealth — invoked by the old Norse for riches, a natural high-roller banker at any table.

candidates.name: Hel
candidates.one_line: Half-corpse, half-living queen of the inglorious dead who named her price for Baldr's release — every thing in creation must weep — and collected when one giantess refused.

candidates.name: Skaði
candidates.one_line: Ski-striding giantess-turned-goddess of the hunt who marched armed into Asgard to demand wergild and chose her husband by his feet alone.

candidates.name: Iðunn
candidates.one_line: Keeper of the apples of youth whose single kidnapping made the entire pantheon start visibly aging — the gods' immortality is a supply chain with one point of failure.

candidates.name: Mímir
candidates.one_line: Beheaded wisdom-keeper whose preserved talking head Odin consults at the well of knowledge — an oracle you can carry.

candidates.name: Gefjon
candidates.one_line: Plow-goddess who won the island of Zealand from King Gylfi on a land-wager — 'as much as four oxen plow in a day' — then cheated magnificently with ox-shaped giant sons.

candidates.name: Surtr
candidates.one_line: Fire giant of Muspelheim whose flaming sword burns the whole world at Ragnarök — the house-burner every god is playing against.

candidates.name: Ymir
candidates.one_line: Primordial frost giant butchered by Odin and his brothers to build the world from his corpse — sky from skull, sea from blood.

candidates.name: Útgarða-Loki
candidates.one_line: Illusionist giant-king who ran the most famous rigged casino in myth: Thor unknowingly drank the ocean, wrestled Old Age, and raced Thought — and lost every game.

candidates.name: Grendel
candidates.one_line: Mead-hall-stalking night terror of Beowulf, kin of Cain, immune to blades and torn apart at the shoulder barehanded.

candidates.name: Fenrir
candidates.one_line: The bound wolf who grows toward the day he swallows Odin whole — every fetter he breaks makes the gods more desperate.

candidates.name: Jörmungandr
candidates.one_line: The Midgard Serpent that circles the world biting its own tail; when it lets go, the end has started.

candidates.name: Sleipnir
candidates.one_line: Odin's eight-legged grey steed, fastest of all horses, born of Loki's most embarrassing shapeshifting escapade.

candidates.name: Fáfnir
candidates.one_line: Dwarf who murdered for cursed gold and warped into the archetypal treasure-hoarding dragon — greed made literal and scaled.

candidates.name: Ratatoskr
candidates.one_line: Gossip-carrying squirrel who runs the world-tree trunk ferrying insults between the eagle at the crown and the dragon at the roots.

candidates.name: Huginn and Muninn
candidates.one_line: Thought and Memory, Odin's raven informants who fly the whole world daily and whisper everything into his ear — the house's surveillance system.

candidates.name: Sigurd (Siegfried)
candidates.one_line: Dragon-slayer who tasted Fafnir's heart-blood, understood bird-speech, claimed the cursed Rhinegold, and was destroyed by it — Wagner's entire Ring runs on him.

candidates.name: Brynhildr
candidates.one_line: Valkyrie punished with mortal sleep behind a wall of flame, whose betrayed oath to Sigurd pulls two dynasties into the fire with her.

candidates.name: Beowulf
candidates.one_line: Geatish strongman who fights Grendel weaponless as a point of sportsmanship, dives a haunted mere, and dies old killing his own dragon.

candidates.name: Wayland the Smith (Völundr)
candidates.one_line: Hamstrung master smith enslaved by a king, who forged his revenge and flew away on wings of his own making — the Germanic Daedalus with a body count.

candidates.name: Valkyries
candidates.one_line: Odin's shield-maidens who choose which fighters die and carry them to Valhalla — the house's talent scouts for the slain.

candidates.name: Norns
candidates.one_line: Three fate-weavers at the well beneath the world-tree who set every being's luck and lifespan — the oddsmakers even gods cannot appeal.

candidates.name: Hamingja
candidates.one_line: A person's heritable luck made into an attendant spirit — Norse fortune as a transferable, spendable asset, practically casino-native.

candidates.name: Draugr
candidates.one_line: Bloated, grave-mound-dwelling undead with unnatural strength who guard their buried treasure and hate the living for having any.

candidates.name: Trolls
candidates.one_line: Mountain- and bridge-haunting brutes of Scandinavian folklore, dim, strong, and famously turned to stone by sunlight.

candidates.name: Krampus
candidates.one_line: Horned Alpine punisher who accompanies St. Nicholas with chains and a birch switch to haul the naughty away in a basket.

candidates.name: Mjölnir
candidates.one_line: Thor's short-handled thunder hammer that always returns — forged as the winning entry in Loki's head-wager with the dwarves.

candidates.name: Gungnir
candidates.one_line: Odin's spear that never misses and over which unbreakable oaths are sworn — the house contract made of ash and iron.

candidates.name: Gleipnir
candidates.one_line: The silk-thin fetter forged from six impossible things (a cat's footfall, a mountain's roots...) that holds Fenrir when iron could not.

candidates.name: Brísingamen
candidates.one_line: Freyja's dwarf-forged golden necklace, the most coveted treasure in the nine worlds and the object of Loki's most famous theft.

candidates.name: Bifröst
candidates.one_line: The burning rainbow bridge between the mortal world and Asgard, guarded by Heimdall and fated to shatter under the riders of Ragnarök.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this tradition is culturally closed or restricted — the corpus is fully published, richly translated, and public domain at the primary-text level; modern heathen revival groups do not restrict depiction (they are flagged 'living' for owner review, and co-opted-symbol cautions are in sensitivity_notes). D-4 exclusions applied: semi-historical and euhemerized humans were excluded as entities — Ragnar Lothbrok (semi-legendary Viking king, story-only legend cycle), Hengist and Horsa (possibly historical Anglo-Saxon founders), saga protagonists like Egill Skallagrímsson and historical kings (Harald Fairhair etc.), and Snorri's euhemerized 'historical Odin the migration chieftain' framing from the Heimskringla prologue (the god Odin is included; the euhemerized human is not). Their legend cycles remain usable as story-only material. Honest omissions rather than guesses: Eostre/Ostara was left off the candidate list because her existence rests on a single line in Bede and reconstruction is contested; minor deities (Bragi, Ullr, Forseti, Rán, Sif, Víðarr, Gná, Hermóðr the messenger, dísir, landvættir, álfar, Nerthus and Tacitus's Tuisto/Mannus) exist with solid attestation and are available for Wave 2 expansion — the 40-cap, not source poverty, excluded them. Source quality 5 supports deep later-wave extraction.

candidates.name: Māui
candidates.one_line: Trickster demigod who fished islands up from the sea, snared the sun with his grandmother's jawbone, and died staking his life against the death goddess herself.

candidates.name: Pele
candidates.one_line: Volcano goddess who births new land in fire, appears as a beautiful stranger or an old woman on the road, and still receives offerings at Kīlauea's rim.

candidates.name: Tangaroa
candidates.one_line: Vast atua of the ocean and all its swarming children, locked in eternal grievance against the forest for sheltering runaway fish-kin.

candidates.name: Tāne
candidates.one_line: Forest father who pushed sky and earth apart with his legs and climbed the twelfth heaven to bring back the three baskets of knowledge.

candidates.name: Hine-nui-te-pō
candidates.one_line: Great woman of the night who fled to the underworld in shame and now crushes to death every soul that tries to pass through her — Māui included.

candidates.name: Ranginui
candidates.one_line: Sky father whose tears for his severed wife Papatūānuku still fall as rain.

candidates.name: Papatūānuku
candidates.one_line: Earth mother from whose embrace all the atua were pried, and to whom all the dead return.

candidates.name: Tūmatauenga
candidates.one_line: War god who alone stood unbowed against the storm and ate his brothers' children — ancestor and template of fighting humankind.

candidates.name: Tāwhirimātea
candidates.one_line: Storm god who sided with his parents against the separation and wages hurricane vengeance on his brothers forever.

candidates.name: Rongo
candidates.one_line: Atua of cultivated food and peace, patron of the kūmara field and the truce.

candidates.name: Rūaumoko
candidates.one_line: The unborn god still turning in his mother the earth's womb — every earthquake is his stirring.

candidates.name: Mahuika
candidates.one_line: Fire goddess conned out of her burning fingernails one by one by Māui until, ruined and raging, she flung her last fire into the trees.

candidates.name: Hina
candidates.one_line: Pan-Polynesian goddess of the moon and tapa-beating, sister-wife-mother of the Māui cycle across a dozen archipelagos.

candidates.name: Kāne
candidates.one_line: Foremost of the four great Hawaiian akua — giver of sunlight, fresh water, and life, to whom no human sacrifice could be offered.

candidates.name: Kū
candidates.one_line: Hawaiian war god of the feather-wrapped snarling images, the one akua whose temples demanded human blood.

candidates.name: Lono
candidates.one_line: God of peace, rain, and the Makahiki season, when war ceased and the islands turned to games, boxing, sledding — and heavy wagering on all of it.

candidates.name: Kanaloa
candidates.one_line: God of the deep ocean and squid, dark complement to Kāne on every voyage and awa-drinking.

candidates.name: Laka
candidates.one_line: Goddess of the hula and the forest greenery laid on her altar — altars still kept in hula hālau today.

candidates.name: Kamapuaʻa
candidates.one_line: Shapeshifting hog-man demigod of rain and wild valleys who wooed and warred with Pele herself.

candidates.name: Haumea
candidates.one_line: Hawaiian goddess of birth who renews herself from age to youth and mothers strange children from parts of her own body — now also a dwarf planet's namesake.

candidates.name: Taʻaroa
candidates.one_line: Tahitian creator who sat alone in his cosmic shell for ages, then cracked it open and built the world from his own body.

candidates.name: ʻOro
candidates.one_line: Tahitian god of war and peace whose red-feather girdle crowned kings and whose Arioi society toured the islands as sacred performers.

candidates.name: Tagaloa
candidates.one_line: Samoan supreme sky-dweller who sent the plover down to find land and shaped people from writhing maggots of the ground.

candidates.name: Whiro
candidates.one_line: Lord of darkness and evil, defeated in the contest for the baskets of knowledge and banished below, where he gnaws toward the day he devours the world.

candidates.name: Ponaturi
candidates.one_line: Pale sea-goblins who sleep ashore but die at the touch of sunlight — exterminated whole houses at a time by heroes who propped the doors open till dawn.

candidates.name: Tāwhaki
candidates.one_line: Lightning-wreathed hero who climbed the vine to the highest heaven to avenge his father and win back his celestial wife.

candidates.name: Rātā
candidates.one_line: Canoe-builder whose felled tree stood whole again each morning until he apologized to the forest's offended spirits.

candidates.name: Kupe
candidates.one_line: Great navigator who chased the giant octopus across the open Pacific and so found Aotearoa, the land of the long white cloud.

candidates.name: Taniwha
candidates.one_line: Water-lurking guardians and man-eaters of river bends and harbor mouths — protectors to their own people, monsters to trespassers.

candidates.name: Te Wheke-a-Muturangi
candidates.one_line: The monstrous octopus that stole Kupe's bait and was pursued across an ocean to its death in Cook Strait.

candidates.name: Pouākai
candidates.one_line: Man-eating mountain eagle that carried off villagers whole — folk memory, plausibly, of the real Haast's eagle.

candidates.name: Kurangaituku
candidates.one_line: The bird-woman ogress with spear-point lips who kept a captive man among her pets until he burned her treasures and fled.

candidates.name: Moʻo
candidates.one_line: Giant black water-lizard spirits of Hawaiian pools and fishponds, guardians who drown the disrespectful.

candidates.name: Menehune
candidates.one_line: Hawaii's hidden little people, master builders who raise a fishpond or heiau in a single night — or abandon it forever at first light.

candidates.name: Patupaiarehe
candidates.one_line: Pale, red-haired fairy folk of the mists and ridgelines who fear cooked food and lure mortals with flute song.

candidates.name: Huakaʻi pō (Night Marchers)
candidates.one_line: Ghostly processions of ancient warriors drumming along old Hawaiian paths by night — look upon them and die, unless an ancestor walks among them.

candidates.name: ʻAumākua
candidates.one_line: Family ancestral guardians who take the body of a shark, owl, or lizard to warn and protect their descendants.

candidates.name: Manaiakalani (Māui's fishhook)
candidates.one_line: The great hook — in many tellings carved from his grandmother's jawbone — that dragged whole islands up from the seafloor; a named Hawaiian star line to this day.

candidates.name: Ngā Kete o te Wānanga
candidates.one_line: The three baskets of knowledge — sacred, ancestral, and worldly lore — carried down from the highest heaven by Tāne.

closed_material_notes: CLOSED/RESTRICTED — reported, not collected: (1) Io-matua-kore, the esoteric Māori supreme being, was whare-wānanga (priestly school) knowledge deliberately withheld from open circulation, and its authenticity/antiquity is itself contested in scholarship — not listed as a candidate. (2) Whakapapa (genealogy) is taonga and tapu: figures who function as real iwi ancestors are excluded under D-4 and tapu together — Paikea the whale rider (Ngāti Porou ancestor; Whale Rider fame), Hatupatu (Te Arawa; the Kurangaituku tale's human protagonist), Hinemoa and Tūtānekai (Te Arawa ancestral romance), and the named migration-canoe captains. Their story cycles are story-only material and any use should involve iwi consultation. (3) Deified historical humans excluded per D-4: Nafanua (Samoan war goddess, widely regarded as a deified historical woman of the Sāfata/Falealupo lineage) and the Captain Cook-received-as-Lono episode (historical human). (4) Specific ʻaumākua identities are private family matters in Hawaiian culture — only the generic class is listed, flagged living+restricted. (5) Hula and Pele chant repertoires are partly hālau-held (transmitted within schools, not freely publishable); the deepest karakia and tohunga lore were never published. (6) Tā moko designs and specific named iwi taniwha/kaitiaki are identity property of particular iwi and hapū — the generic taniwha class is listed restricted, individual named guardians are not. (7) General posture: NZ Māori commentators have repeatedly and publicly objected to uncredited game use of Māori imagery (H1Z1 'Maui' 2019, later cases through 2025); the owner should treat every Māori-specific asset as consultation-recommended even where technically public-domain published.

candidates.name: Olodumare (Olorun)
candidates.one_line: The remote supreme owner of the heavens who delegates the world to the orishas and rarely descends to the table himself.

candidates.name: Obatala
candidates.one_line: White-clad sculptor of human bodies who, drunk on palm wine, shaped imperfect people and swore off wine to become their patron.

candidates.name: Orunmila
candidates.one_line: Witness of destiny and master of Ifá divination — the one being who already knows every player's odds.

candidates.name: Eshu (Elegba/Eleguá)
candidates.one_line: Trickster of the crossroads, divine messenger and lord of chance who must be paid first or every message and every bet goes wrong.

candidates.name: Shango (Ṣàngó)
candidates.one_line: Thunder-hurling storm king of Oyo whose cult cries 'Oba ko so — the king did not hang' against the myth of his own undoing.

candidates.name: Ogun
candidates.one_line: Iron-toothed god of the forge, war, and every blade and engine — first orisha to cut a path down to earth.

candidates.name: Yemoja (Yemayá)
candidates.one_line: Great mother of waters and of all orishas, whose skirts are the surface of the sea.

candidates.name: Oshun (Ọ̀ṣun)
candidates.one_line: Honey-sweet river goddess of love, luxury, and fresh water whose withdrawal once made the whole world barren — her Osogbo grove is a living UNESCO site.

candidates.name: Oya
candidates.one_line: Whirlwind warrior of storms and the Niger river who guards the gates of the cemetery with nine heads of the flood.

candidates.name: Olokun
candidates.one_line: Androgynous sovereign of the abyssal ocean's wealth who challenged the supreme god to a contest of splendor and was tricked into losing by a chameleon.

candidates.name: Babalú-Ayé (Shopona)
candidates.one_line: Feared lord of smallpox and plague, exiled by the other orishas to wander the roads as a leprous beggar before his triumphant return — his Yoruba name was taboo to speak.

candidates.name: Osanyin
candidates.one_line: One-legged, one-armed, one-eyed keeper of every leaf's medicine, who speaks in a bird's falsetto.

candidates.name: Ochosi (Oṣóòsì)
candidates.one_line: Unerring hunter of the single arrow, patron of trackers and of justice that always finds its mark.

candidates.name: Ibeji
candidates.one_line: The deified sacred twins whose carved figures are cradled and fed when one twin dies — luck incarnate in duplicate.

candidates.name: Aje
candidates.one_line: Orisha of money, markets, and material fortune — the literal cash-flow deity of the pantheon.

candidates.name: Oduduwa
candidates.one_line: Divine progenitor who climbed down a chain to found Ile-Ife on a handful of sand and a five-toed chicken — every Yoruba king claims his blood.

candidates.name: Nana Buluku
candidates.one_line: Primordial grandmother older than the orishas themselves, mother of the moon and sun in Fon tradition.

candidates.name: Mawu-Lisa
candidates.one_line: Fon twin creator — cool moon-mother Mawu and hot sun-father Lisa fused into one balancing deity.

candidates.name: Legba
candidates.one_line: Fon-Vodun opener of the gate and interpreter between gods and mortals, ancestor of the crossroads-deal legend.

candidates.name: Dan (Aido-Hwedo)
candidates.one_line: Rainbow serpent who carried the creator in its mouth and now coils beneath the earth holding the world together.

candidates.name: Nyame
candidates.one_line: Akan sky god who once owned every story in the world and set the price that Anansi paid.

candidates.name: Ala (Ani)
candidates.one_line: Igbo earth mother, womb and tomb at once, whose laws define every abomination against the land.

candidates.name: Amadioha
candidates.one_line: Igbo thunder god of justice whose white ram and lightning strike down oath-breakers.

candidates.name: Bayajidda
candidates.one_line: Hausa founding hero who slew the snake in the well of Daura and fathered the seven Hausa states.

candidates.name: Ozidi
candidates.one_line: Ijaw epic avenger raised by his sorceress grandmother to destroy the assassins of his father — hero of a seven-day performed saga.

candidates.name: Adze
candidates.one_line: Ewe vampire that flies as a firefly light, slips through keyholes, and drinks sleepers dry.

candidates.name: Sasabonsam
candidates.one_line: Akan forest horror with iron teeth and hooked legs that dangles from cotton trees to snatch hunters.

candidates.name: Ninki Nanka
candidates.one_line: Gambian swamp dragon whose mere sight is said to kill within weeks — West Africa's flagship cryptid.

candidates.name: Bultungin (werehyena)
candidates.one_line: Kanuri and Sahelian shapeshifter — 'I change myself into a hyena' — the region's answer to the werewolf.

candidates.name: Mami Wata
candidates.one_line: Mirror-and-comb water spirit, half woman half serpent-fish, who trades wealth and beauty for devotion across the whole Atlantic coast.

candidates.name: Abiku / Ogbanje
candidates.one_line: Spirit-children born only to die and return again and again, tormenting the same mother — famous through Achebe and Emezi.

candidates.name: Aziza
candidates.one_line: Tiny Fon forest people who gave humanity fire and hunting magic from their anthill homes.

candidates.name: Egungun
candidates.one_line: The masked collective ancestral dead who return in whirling cloth masquerades to judge the living — the public spectacle is famous, the society behind it is closed.

candidates.name: Anansi
candidates.one_line: Akan spider trickster who won all the world's stories from the sky god by capturing impossible creatures as his stake.

candidates.name: Ijapa (Àjàpá) the Tortoise
candidates.one_line: Yoruba trickster tortoise of hundreds of folktales, forever scheming, forever cracking his own shell.

candidates.name: Mmoatia
candidates.one_line: Backward-footed Akan forest dwarfs, mischief-makers and herb-teachers — one of the creatures Anansi staked in his story-wager.

candidates.name: Oshe Shango
candidates.one_line: Shango's double-headed axe, carried as dance-wand and lightning conductor by his devotees.

candidates.name: Opon Ifá
candidates.one_line: The carved divination tray on which the babalawo marks the odu — the interface between fate and the table.

candidates.name: The Golden Stool (Sika Dwa Kofi)
candidates.one_line: The Ashanti royal stool said to have descended from the sky containing the soul of the whole nation — no one, not even the king, may sit on it.

closed_material_notes: CLOSED/RESTRICTED (reported, not collected): (1) The Ifá literary corpus — the 256 odu and their thousands of verses — is orally transmitted and initiation-bound; the highest material, Igba Odu (the sacred calabash of Odu), is reserved for fully initiated chief babalawos and per the tradition is not to be seen or published. Orunmila and the public frame of Ifá are listed; verse-level content should not be mined. (2) Egungun and Oro society inner knowledge (masquerade construction, society rites) is secret-society material; only the widely published public face is listed, flagged restricted. (3) The Ogboni society's rites and emblems (edan) are initiation-restricted. (4) Shopona's Yoruba name carried a spoken taboo; the diaspora name Babalú-Ayé is the safe public handle. (5) The Golden Stool is a living sacred royal object of the Ashanti monarchy — listed flagged restricted for owner review. D-4 EXCLUSIONS (real/likely-historical humans, story-only material): Sundiata Keita (historical founder of the Mali Empire; the Epic of Sundiata is superb story material but he is excluded as an entity), Queen Amina of Zazzau (historical Hausa warrior queen), Moremi Ajasoro (semi-historical Ife heroine), and the historical Alaafins of Oyo. BORDERLINE INCLUDED: Shango and Oduduwa carry euhemeristic traditions identifying them with early kings of Oyo/Ife; historicity is unverifiable and both are worshipped today purely as orishas, so they are listed as gods — flagged here for owner awareness. IP NOTE: modern depictions of Anansi (American Gods, Marvel) and the orishas (e.g., Riot Games' unreleased projects, comics) are modern IP; only the public-domain folk/religious substrate is licensed-free.

candidates.name: Viracocha
candidates.one_line: Staff-bearing creator who made the world at Lake Titicaca, wandered it as a ragged beggar, then walked away across the Pacific — later demoted in his own empire in favor of the Sun.

candidates.name: Inti
candidates.one_line: Imperial sun god and divine ancestor of the Inca dynasty, whose golden face still blazes on Andean flags and whose Inti Raymi festival is re-enacted in Cusco every June.

candidates.name: Pachamama
candidates.one_line: Earth Mother who is the literal living soil — still fed chicha, coca, and despacho bundles by millions of Andeans, entwined with the Virgin Mary.

candidates.name: Mama Quilla
candidates.one_line: Silver-faced moon goddess, wife of the sun and regulator of the ritual calendar, defended with noise-making armies during eclipses when a jaguar tried to devour her.

candidates.name: Illapa
candidates.one_line: Thunder god who cracks his sling across the sky and pours rain from his sister's celestial water jar — chief weather power of the empire.

candidates.name: Mama Cocha
candidates.one_line: Mother Sea, sovereign of ocean, lakes, and fish, placated by coastal fishermen for calm waters and full nets.

candidates.name: Pachacamac
candidates.one_line: Coastal 'World-Shaker' whose buried oracle city drew pilgrims for a millennium — turn your face from him and he answers with earthquakes.

candidates.name: Supay
candidates.one_line: Lord of Ukhu Pacha, the world below, and its horde of demons — fused with the Christian Devil yet still danced for and bribed in the Diablada and by miners at the mountain's mouth.

candidates.name: Kon
candidates.one_line: Boneless, weightless god of wind and rain who first peopled the desert coast, then stripped it to sand in fury when his creations turned to his rival.

candidates.name: Ekeko
candidates.one_line: Mustachioed pot-bellied god of abundance and luck, loaded with miniature wishes at the Alasitas fair and paid in cigarettes and banknotes every Friday — a living luck cult.

candidates.name: Chaska
candidates.one_line: 'The long-haired star' — Venus as dawn goddess, page of the sun and patroness of girls and flowers.

candidates.name: Catequil
candidates.one_line: Thunder-and-oracle god of the northern highlands whose famous shrine prophesied against Atahualpa — who smashed the idol and burned the holy hill in revenge.

candidates.name: Urcuchillay
candidates.one_line: Multicolored llama-shaped constellation god (our Lyra) who watches over the herds and the herders who watch him back.

candidates.name: Thunupa
candidates.one_line: Aymara weather-and-volcano god of the Altiplano, a wandering bringer of storms sometimes read into the Staff God of Tiwanaku's Sun Gate.

candidates.name: Ai Apaec
candidates.one_line: Fanged Moche 'Decapitator' — spider, serpent, and mountain god who took prisoners' severed heads in tribute; the great monster-slaying hero of Moche pot-paintings.

candidates.name: Si
candidates.one_line: Chimú moon god who outranked the sun on the desert coast — the moon commands the tides and can be seen by night and day alike.

candidates.name: Pariacaca
candidates.one_line: Five-in-one falcon-born mountain and storm god of the Huarochirí myths who drowned a corrupt village with red and yellow rain — his glacier peak still receives veneration as an apu.

candidates.name: Cuniraya Viracocha
candidates.one_line: Trickster-creator of the Huarochirí Manuscript who roams in beggar's rags, impregnates a virgin goddess with a fruit, and blesses or curses every animal he meets on the road.

candidates.name: Mama Sara
candidates.one_line: Maize Mother embodied in strangely twinned corn ears, dressed as dolls and kept for a year to guarantee the harvest.

candidates.name: Chaupiñamca
candidates.one_line: Great female huaca of Huarochirí, sister-counterpart to Pariacaca, a five-armed stone goddess whose festival was danced 'to make her rejoice'.

candidates.name: Wakon
candidates.one_line: Cave-dwelling cannibal demon who tried to devour the orphaned children of the sun in the Willkas myth — the Andean ogre of the dark hearth.

candidates.name: Anchanchu
candidates.one_line: Grinning Aymara spirit of caves, rivers, and lonely places that befriends travelers only to drain their blood and health.

candidates.name: Manco Capac
candidates.one_line: Legendary son of the Sun who rose from Lake Titicaca (or the cave of Pacaritambo) with a golden staff to found Cusco where it sank into the earth.

candidates.name: Mama Ocllo
candidates.one_line: Sister-wife of Manco Capac who taught the arts of weaving and civilization to the peoples of the valley — co-founder of the imperial line.

candidates.name: Huatya Curi
candidates.one_line: 'Potato-eater' — a ragged nobody who won a rich man's daughter by beating his sneering brother-in-law in escalating wagers of drinking, dancing, building, and beast-parading.

candidates.name: Ayar Cachi
candidates.one_line: Mightiest of the four founding Ayar brothers, whose sling-shots split hills — so his frightened siblings tricked him back into the origin cave and sealed him in forever.

candidates.name: Amaru
candidates.one_line: Double-headed serpent-dragon of the underworld that bursts from lakes and springs when the world-order turns over, arcing between worlds as the rainbow.

candidates.name: Ccoa
candidates.one_line: Flying storm-cat with hail streaming from its eyes and tail — the feared feline servant of the mountain lords who ruins crops for the unpious.

candidates.name: Apu
candidates.one_line: The mountain lords themselves — each great peak a named, ranked, living ancestor-spirit still fed coca and despachos by the communities under its shadow.

candidates.name: Muki
candidates.one_line: Bearded dwarf of the mine tunnels who trades rich veins for offerings and collapses shafts on the greedy — Andean miners still cut deals with him.

candidates.name: Pishtaco
candidates.one_line: Pale night-stalker who harvests the body fat of Andean travelers — a colonial-trauma boogeyman that keeps updating itself (friars, surgeons, gringos).

candidates.name: Sirena
candidates.one_line: Enchanted mermaid of highland lakes and waterfalls who tunes a musician's charango overnight — supernatural skill granted, at the price of a pact.

candidates.name: Condenado
candidates.one_line: A damned soul refused by both worlds, doomed to walk the passes crunching bones — meeting one on a night road is the classic Andean horror tale.

candidates.name: Punchao
candidates.one_line: The golden boy-shaped sun idol of Cusco's Coricancha, fed daily and housing the ashes of dead Inca kings' hearts — carried into the jungle by the last free Inca and lost.

candidates.name: Tapac-yauri
candidates.one_line: The golden staff of Manco Capac that sank effortlessly into the earth at the destined site of Cusco — the empire's founding dowsing rod.

candidates.name: Lanzón
candidates.one_line: The fanged granite blade-idol still standing in the black labyrinth under Chavín de Huántar, an oracle stone that WAS its god, older than every empire that followed.

closed_material_notes: D-4 exclusions (real historical humans, story-only material): the Sapa Incas — Pachacuti (who reorganized the state cult), Huayna Capac, Atahualpa (smasher of Catequil's oracle), Túpac Amaru — are historical rulers with rich legend cycles usable as story material only, not entities. Inkarri, the messianic 'the Inca will return when his buried head regrows a body' myth, is a mythified composite of the executed historical Incas (Atahualpa/Túpac Amaru) and is also politically live in Peru — recorded here as story-only material rather than listed as an entity. Manco Capac and Mama Ocllo ARE listed, since mainstream scholarship treats them as mythical progenitors (children of Inti), not attested humans. Closed/practitioner-held material NOT collected: the specific contents and sequences of despacho ceremonies, paqo/altomisayoq initiation knowledge (Q'ero and other communities transmit this orally to initiates), and curandero plant-ceremony knowledge — all overlap living practice and are held by practitioners, not the public sphere; only the widely published outer facts (that such offerings exist) inform the tradition record. Pishtaco is included but flagged 'restricted' for owner review: it encodes real, ongoing indigenous fears of outsider violence and has fueled actual mob incidents, so its use as a mere monster skin deserves a deliberate owner decision.

candidates.name: Ukko
candidates.one_line: Finnish supreme sky-and-thunder god whose hammer-strike opens the rains; his name still lives in the Finnish word for thunderstorm, ukkonen.

candidates.name: Väinämöinen
candidates.one_line: Ancient sage born old, whose songs reshape reality; sails away at the epic's end promising to return when he is needed — the model for Tolkien's Gandalf.

candidates.name: Ilmarinen
candidates.one_line: The eternal smith who hammered out the sky-dome itself and forged the Sampo — and once forged himself a wife of gold who was too cold to love.

candidates.name: Lemminkäinen
candidates.one_line: Reckless lover-warrior hacked to pieces in the river of Tuonela and stitched back to life by his mother with a rake and bee-brought honey.

candidates.name: Kullervo
candidates.one_line: Doom-cursed slave of superhuman strength who ruins everything he touches and dies on his own talking sword — the direct model for Tolkien's Túrin.

candidates.name: Louhi
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting mistress of Pohjola who steals the sun and moon, becomes a giant eagle in battle, and loses the Sampo and her wealth to the sea.

candidates.name: Joukahainen
candidates.one_line: Brash young rival sung neck-deep into a swamp by Väinämöinen, who buys his life by promising his own sister and later turns crossbow assassin.

candidates.name: Aino
candidates.one_line: The maiden promised to old Väinämöinen who walks into the sea rather than wed him, returning as an uncatchable fish.

candidates.name: Ilmatar
candidates.one_line: Virgin spirit of the air on whose knee the world-egg was laid and broken, creating heaven and earth; mother of Väinämöinen.

candidates.name: Tapio
candidates.one_line: Moss-bearded king of the forest whose favor hunters begged at every treeline; his household governs all game in the woods.

candidates.name: Mielikki
candidates.one_line: Mistress of the forest and healer of animals' paws, who grants or withholds hunting fortune; borrowed wholesale as a D&D goddess.

candidates.name: Ahti
candidates.one_line: Bearded god of waters and fish, lord of the deeps to whom Finnish fishermen sacrificed for a full net.

candidates.name: Vellamo
candidates.one_line: Ahti's queen of the waves, invoked for calm seas; her name still graces Finnish ships and a museum.

candidates.name: Tuoni
candidates.one_line: Grim lord of Tuonela, the island land of the dead ringed by a black river — visited by heroes, left by almost none.

candidates.name: Loviatar
candidates.one_line: Blind daughter of Death impregnated by the wind, who birthed the nine diseases; her name was lifted for D&D's goddess of pain.

candidates.name: Hiisi
candidates.one_line: Demon of the sacred groves turned malevolent goblin-lord; his giant elk leads hunters on ruinous chases.

candidates.name: Ajatar
candidates.one_line: Evil forest spirit of Finnish lore, a serpent-suckling 'devil of the woods' who spreads sickness to any who look on her.

candidates.name: Iku-Turso
candidates.one_line: Ancient sea-ogre raised from the depths to fight Väinämöinen, plucked out by the ears and sworn back beneath the waves forever.

candidates.name: Surma
candidates.one_line: The embodiment of sudden violent death guarding Tuonela's gate — all snapping jaws, freezing whomever it fixes with its gaze.

candidates.name: Antero Vipunen
candidates.one_line: Dead giant shaman sleeping under the earth, trees growing from his jaw; Väinämöinen climbs into his belly to pry loose the lost words of power.

candidates.name: Näkki
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting water spirit lurking under piers and bridges to drag the unwary — especially children — beneath the surface.

candidates.name: Otso
candidates.one_line: The sacred bear, 'golden apple of the forest,' whose true name could not be spoken and whose skull was honored in ritual feast — heart of the old Finnish bear cult.

candidates.name: Sampo
candidates.one_line: The many-lidded magic mill that grinds out grain, salt, and gold from nothing — fought over, shattered at sea, its fragments seeding the world's fortune.

candidates.name: Kantele of Väinämöinen
candidates.one_line: Harp built from a giant pike's jawbone whose music lulls every living thing to sleep — Finland's national instrument in mythic form.

candidates.name: Dievas
candidates.one_line: Serene Baltic sky-father (Latvian Dievs) who rides down the world-hill to bless the fields — the calm summit of the Baltic pantheon.

candidates.name: Perkūnas
candidates.one_line: Baltic thunder god (Latvian Pērkons) whose axe hunts devils across the sky; the most vigorously worshipped god of the last pagan corner of Europe.

candidates.name: Saulė
candidates.one_line: The sun as a radiant mother goddess driving her copper-wheeled chariot over the world-tree, weeping red berries for the dead.

candidates.name: Mėnuo
candidates.one_line: The moon-god (Latvian Mēness) who cheated on the Sun with the morning star and was hacked apart by Perkūnas — why the moon wanes still.

candidates.name: Laima
candidates.one_line: Baltic goddess of fate and luck who pronounces each newborn's fortune at the hearth — her very name means 'luck', and Latvia's most famous chocolate brand still bears it.

candidates.name: Dalia
candidates.one_line: Lithuanian allotter of each person's dalis — their share of wealth and goods — the goddess who deals out and repossesses material fortune.

candidates.name: Žemyna
candidates.one_line: Lithuanian earth-mother to whom beer was poured and bread kissed; every harvest begins and ends with her blessing.

candidates.name: Gabija
candidates.one_line: Lithuanian spirit of the sacred hearth-fire, 'put to bed' each night with salt and clean water — insult her and she walks the house as flame.

candidates.name: Aušrinė
candidates.one_line: The morning star as a beautiful goddess of dawn and love whose affair with the Moon set off a divine dismemberment.

candidates.name: Velnias
candidates.one_line: The Baltic devil-trickster of bogs and the underworld (Latvian Velns), eternal quarry of Perkūnas's thunder-axe and keeper of the dead.

candidates.name: Ragana
candidates.one_line: The Baltic witch — 'the seeing one' — a once-divine crone of death and regeneration demoted to hag of the fairy tales.

candidates.name: Aitvaras
candidates.one_line: Lithuanian household spirit — rooster indoors, fiery serpent aloft — that steals grain and coin from the neighbors to enrich its keeper; luck that never comes clean.

candidates.name: Kaukas
candidates.one_line: Small chthonic household spirit of Lithuanian lore that brings quiet prosperity to families who feed and clothe it.

candidates.name: Jumis
candidates.one_line: Latvian field-spirit of the double ear of grain — finding his twinned stalk at harvest is the luck of the whole coming year.

candidates.name: Eglė the Queen of Serpents
candidates.one_line: Lithuanian bride of the serpent-king Žilvinas who, after his betrayal and death, turns herself and her children into trees — the most famous of all Baltic tales.

candidates.name: Jūratė
candidates.one_line: Amber-palace goddess of the Baltic Sea whose love for a mortal fisherman brought Perkūnas's bolt down on her home — amber on the beach is her palace's wreckage.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this family is culturally closed or depiction-restricted: Finnish, Lithuanian, and Latvian folk material is fully published, public-sphere, and the revival communities (Romuva, Dievturība, Finnish native faith) publish and promote it openly — 'living' flags are for owner review of on-screen roles, not restriction. Exclusions applied instead: (1) D-4 — no real historical humans were listed; the legend cycles of Lithuanian Grand Dukes (e.g. the Šventaragis funeral-valley legend, the Gediminas iron-wolf dream) and the Sovijus soul-journey tale (attested 1261, framed around a mortal man) are story-only material usable for events/lore, not entities. (2) Authenticity screen — 19th-century romantic-nationalist inventions with no folk attestation (e.g. the 'love goddess' Milda, much of the chronicler-invented pantheon of Theodor Narbutt) were excluded rather than padded in; some minor names retained (Dalia, Kaukas, Jumis) are genuinely attested but thin, and are scored at the honest floor accordingly. (3) The Kalevala is Lönnrot's literary redaction — entries flagged ip:public_domain_literary lean on the epic frame (PD in the Crawford/Kirby translations), while the underlying runo-song tradition is folklore; later waves should cite runo variants, not only the epic, for myth details.

candidates.name: Big Bad Wolf
candidates.one_line: The composite devourer of Red Riding Hood and the Three Little Pigs — folklore's universal predator at the door.

candidates.name: Cinderella
candidates.one_line: ATU 510A's persecuted heroine, raised from the ashes by magic to outshine her tormentors at the ball.

candidates.name: Little Red Riding Hood
candidates.one_line: ATU 333's hooded girl on the forest path, forever one question away from the wolf's teeth.

candidates.name: Jack the Giant-Killer
candidates.one_line: The beanstalk-climbing trickster-everyman of English Jack tales who robs and topples giants.

candidates.name: Hansel and Gretel
candidates.one_line: The abandoned sibling pair of ATU 327A who out-cunning the cannibal witch — breadcrumbs, cage, and oven.

candidates.name: Gingerbread Witch
candidates.one_line: The child-eating witch of the edible house — the archetypal witch-in-the-woods beyond Baba Yaga.

candidates.name: Rumpelstiltskin
candidates.one_line: The name-hoarding imp of ATU 500 who spins straw to gold and stakes everything on a guessing game.

candidates.name: Bluebeard
candidates.one_line: Perrault's wife-murdering aristocrat with one forbidden door and a key that won't stop bleeding.

candidates.name: The Snow Queen
candidates.one_line: Andersen's glittering sovereign of winter who steals boys' hearts with a mirror-splinter of ice.

candidates.name: Beanstalk Giant
candidates.one_line: The fee-fi-fo-fum ogre above the clouds, hoarding gold, a golden-egg hen, and a singing harp.

candidates.name: Boogeyman
candidates.one_line: The shapeless under-the-bed terror invoked by parents worldwide — fear itself given a closet.

candidates.name: Fairy Godmother
candidates.one_line: The donor-figure of ATU 510 who converts pumpkins and rags into carriages and gowns — with a midnight deadline.

candidates.name: Pied Piper of Hamelin
candidates.one_line: The rainbow-clad rat-charmer whose unpaid invoice cost Hamelin its children — legend possibly shadowing a real 1284 event.

candidates.name: The Frog Prince
candidates.one_line: The enchanted amphibian of ATU 440, redeemable by a princess's reluctant promise (or a well-aimed throw at the wall).

candidates.name: Tom Thumb
candidates.one_line: The thumb-sized trickster hero swallowed by cows and carried off by ravens across ATU 700.

candidates.name: Goldilocks
candidates.one_line: The just-right intruder of the Three Bears' cottage — folklore's patron of trespass and porridge.

candidates.name: Puss in Boots
candidates.one_line: Perrault's booted cat-trickster who cons a kingdom for his penniless master — the Helpful Trickster incarnate.

candidates.name: Reynard the Fox
candidates.one_line: The amoral fox-baron of medieval European beast epic who talks his way out of every gallows.

candidates.name: Br'er Rabbit
candidates.one_line: The tar-baby-punching trickster rabbit of African-American oral tradition — weak against strong, wits against all.

candidates.name: Headless Horseman
candidates.one_line: The decapitated night-rider of Sleepy Hollow, hurling his pumpkin head at those who linger past the bridge.

candidates.name: Bloody Mary
candidates.one_line: The mirror-summoned apparition of the chanted dare — pre-internet slumber-party ritual horror.

candidates.name: Sandman
candidates.one_line: The dream-dust bringer of European nursery lore, with a dark Hoffmann-era eye-stealing shadow.

candidates.name: Jack Frost
candidates.one_line: The window-painting personification of frost, nipping noses across Anglo-American winter lore.

candidates.name: Tooth Fairy
candidates.one_line: The coin-for-teeth night visitor — a living household ritual, if not a worshipped one.

candidates.name: Will-o'-the-Wisp
candidates.one_line: The ghost-light over the marsh that leads travelers off the safe path and into the bog.

candidates.name: Mothman
candidates.one_line: Point Pleasant's red-eyed winged omen of 1966–67, forever tied to the Silver Bridge collapse.

candidates.name: Jersey Devil
candidates.one_line: The Leeds family's cursed thirteenth child — bat-winged, horse-headed haunt of the Pine Barrens since the 1700s.

candidates.name: Chupacabra
candidates.one_line: The goat-sucker of 1990s Puerto Rico and the borderlands, leaving livestock drained through neat punctures.

candidates.name: Kraken
candidates.one_line: The island-sized Scandinavian sea horror whose arms drag whole ships under — giant squid grown to myth.

candidates.name: Loch Ness Monster
candidates.one_line: Nessie, the world's flagship lake monster — from St. Columba's 6th-century account to the 1934 hoax photo.

candidates.name: Bigfoot
candidates.one_line: The Pacific Northwest's giant hairy hominid of plaster casts and shaky film — name rooted in Halkomelem sasq'ets.

candidates.name: Yeti
candidates.one_line: The Abominable Snowman of Himalayan high passes, tracked by mountaineers and rooted in Sherpa lore.

candidates.name: Ogopogo
candidates.one_line: Lake Okanagan's serpent — the tourist-friendly mask over the Syilx water being Naitaka.

candidates.name: Jackalope
candidates.one_line: The antlered rabbit of American tall-tale taxidermy, allegedly lured with whiskey and singing cowboy songs back.

candidates.name: Mokele-mbembe
candidates.one_line: The Congo basin's rumored river giant, beloved of living-dinosaur expeditions since the colonial era.

candidates.name: Beast of Gévaudan
candidates.one_line: The wolf-thing that killed scores in 1760s France and outran the king's own hunters — legend grown from real attacks.

candidates.name: Magic Mirror
candidates.one_line: The wall-bound truth-teller of Snow White that cannot flatter — fairest-of-them-all on demand.

candidates.name: Seven-League Boots
candidates.one_line: The ogre's stolen boots that cross seven leagues a stride — European folklore's fast-travel item.

candidates.name: Glass Slipper
candidates.one_line: The one shoe in the kingdom that fits exactly one foot — Perrault's identity-lock made crystal.

closed_material_notes: Excluded as belonging to living Indigenous traditions with their own protocols (report, don't collect): Thunderbird and Wendigo/Windigo (Algonquian and other First Nations) — sacred and, for Wendigo, subject to speech taboos in some communities; both deferred to a Native American family census rather than treated as generic 'cryptids'. Bunyip and Yowie (Aboriginal Australian origins) deferred to an Australian family for the same reason. Cross-family dedup (not closed, just out of this family's scope): Baba Yaga is already in the Wave 0 calibration set; Anansi (Akan/West African), La Llorona (Mexican), Krampus (Alpine Germanic), leprechaun (Irish), kitsune (Japanese), and golem (Jewish) belong to their regional families. Internet-era creations (Slenderman, Fresno Nightcrawler, SCP-adjacent beings) excluded per family definition; the Flatwoods Monster was considered and dropped as UFO-era rather than traditional folklore. D-4 note: the Pied Piper legend may shadow a real 1284 Hamelin event and the Beast of Gévaudan grew from real 1760s animal attacks, but both listed entities are the legendary figures, not historical humans; no deified historical humans occur in this corpus.

candidates.name: Sedna (Nuliajuk / Arnakuagsak / Nerrivik)
candidates.one_line: The Sea Woman — thrown from her father's kayak, her severed fingers became the seals and whales; she withholds all sea game when angered until a shaman combs her hair.

candidates.name: Sila (Silap Inua)
candidates.one_line: The breath-soul of the world — weather, air, and moral order in one impersonal, all-permeating power that punishes broken taboos with storms.

candidates.name: Nanook (Nanuq)
candidates.one_line: Master of the polar bears, who decides whether hunters deserve their prey and enforces respect for the killed bear.

candidates.name: Igaluk / Taqqiq (the Moon Man)
candidates.one_line: The moon spirit who eternally pursues his sister the Sun across the sky, and who watches over taboo-breakers from above.

candidates.name: Malina / Siqiniq (the Sun Woman)
candidates.one_line: The sun — a woman who fled into the sky bearing a torch, forever chased by her brother the Moon.

candidates.name: Pinga
candidates.one_line: Caribou Inuit goddess of the hunt, fertility, and medicine who escorts the newly dead down to Adlivun.

candidates.name: Torngarsuk (Tornarsuk)
candidates.one_line: The great spirit of Greenland's shamans, chief of the tornait helping-spirits — demonized into 'the Devil' by missionaries and left with almost nothing to lose.

candidates.name: Anguta
candidates.one_line: Sedna's father and the grim ferryman-gatherer who drags the dead down to sleep in Adlivun.

candidates.name: Tekkeitsertok
candidates.one_line: Master of the caribou herds — the inland counterpart to Sedna's dominion over the sea.

candidates.name: Agloolik
candidates.one_line: The spirit dwelling under the sea ice who guides hunters to seal breathing-holes — or capsizes them.

candidates.name: Kiviuq
candidates.one_line: The eternal wanderer — an Inuit Odysseus who has kayaked past sea monsters, bee-women, and cannibal giants for generations and is said to still be traveling.

candidates.name: Atanarjuat (the Fast Runner)
candidates.one_line: Igloolik legend of the naked runner who escaped his ambushers across the spring sea ice — carried to world fame by the 2001 film.

candidates.name: Amarok
candidates.one_line: The gigantic lone wolf that stalks and devours those foolish enough to hunt the tundra alone at night.

candidates.name: Akhlut
candidates.one_line: An orca that walks ashore as a wolf — a shape-shifting predator whose wolf tracks end at the water's edge.

candidates.name: Adlet
candidates.one_line: A bloodthirsty race with human torsos and canine legs, born of a woman and a dog and swifter than any hunter.

candidates.name: Tizheruk
candidates.one_line: A great serpent of Alaskan waters said to snatch people from piers without ever being seen.

candidates.name: Qiqirn
candidates.one_line: A huge, skittish bald dog-spirit whose approach causes fits — but which flees in terror if its name is shouted.

candidates.name: Qalupalik (Qallupilluit)
candidates.one_line: The green-skinned sea hag who hums beneath the ice and carries off children who stray too near the floe edge in her amauti pouch.

candidates.name: Mahaha
candidates.one_line: A giggling, ice-blue demon that tickles its victims to death, leaving them frozen with a twisted smile — outwitted by tricking it to drink from open water.

candidates.name: Ijiraq
candidates.one_line: A caribou-eyed shapeshifter of the between-lands that hides children and can never be looked at directly.

candidates.name: Tuurngait
candidates.one_line: The helping-spirits of the angakkuit (shamans) — nameable powers that can heal, find game, or turn wild and malignant when unbound.

candidates.name: Tariaksuq (shadow people)
candidates.one_line: Half-visible beings who live parallel lives beside humans and can only be seen from the corner of the eye.

candidates.name: Inupasugjuk
candidates.one_line: The slow-witted giants of the tundra who treat captured humans as curious dolls.

candidates.name: Tupilaq
candidates.one_line: A vengeance-fetish assembled from bone, sinew, and grave-parts, sung to life to hunt an enemy — but liable to turn on its maker if the target's magic is stronger.

closed_material_notes: Collected nothing from the genuinely closed layer, per the sensitivity rule: (1) Angakkuq (shaman) craft knowledge — initiation practices, personal secret songs (irinaliutit), and the individually-owned names/pacts of specific tuurngait helping-spirits — was personal, secret knowledge in the tradition itself and is treated by many communities as culturally sensitive; reported here, not extracted (the generic tuurngait CONCEPT is widely published and listed with flag 'restricted'). (2) Tupilaq-making was secret, dangerous knowledge; only the widely-published concept (and the now-commercial Greenlandic carving tradition) is listed, flagged 'restricted'. (3) D-4 exclusions: named historical angakkuit recorded by Rasmussen (e.g. Aua, the poet-shaman Uvavnuk) are real people, not entities — their recorded songs and accounts are story-only material, partly public domain via the Fifth Thule reports. Atanarjuat is likely a legend cycle about real Igloolik ancestors; listed as hero but flagged 'restricted' for owner review on exactly this D-4 boundary, and the film (Isuma, 2001) is modern IP — only the underlying oral legend is usable. (4) Honest zero: no luck/fortune/gambling deity exists in this tradition, so no 'luck_gambling' flags — this is a real absence, not an oversight.

candidates.name: Dangun Wanggeom
candidates.one_line: Grandson of heaven and son of a bear-turned-woman, mythic founder-progenitor of the Korean people, still venerated as part of the Daejongist trinity.

candidates.name: Hwanung
candidates.one_line: Sky prince who descended to the sandalwood tree on Mt. Taebaek with 3,000 spirits and the ministers of wind, rain, and cloud to govern the human world.

candidates.name: Mireuk
candidates.one_line: Giant primordial creator of an age of plenty who lost the world in a rigged flower-growing wager and withdrew, cursing the age that followed.

candidates.name: Seokga
candidates.one_line: Trickster usurper who stole the winning flower while Mireuk slept — won rulership of the human world by cheating at a divine wager.

candidates.name: Cheonjiwang
candidates.one_line: Jeju sky king of the creation epic who descended to earth, sired divine twins, and set them the contest that split the cosmos.

candidates.name: Daebyeolwang
candidates.one_line: Elder twin who rightfully grew the winning flower, was cheated of the living world, and now rules the dead with cold, perfect law.

candidates.name: Sobyeolwang
candidates.one_line: Younger twin who swapped the flowers in his sleep-feigning brother's lap and won a living world he can barely govern — hence its chaos.

candidates.name: Yongwang
candidates.one_line: Dragon King of the undersea palace who commands storms and catches, still fed and flattered in fishing-village gut rituals.

candidates.name: Sansin
candidates.one_line: White-bearded mountain god with a tiger familiar, so entrenched that Buddhist temples keep a shrine hall for him.

candidates.name: Chilseong
candidates.one_line: The Seven Stars of the Big Dipper as a deity of fate, longevity, and fortune — the god you petition when your luck must turn.

candidates.name: Samsin Halmoni
candidates.one_line: Grandmother goddess of childbirth who slaps newborns to make them cry — the blue Mongolian spot is her handprint.

candidates.name: Bari-gongju (Princess Bari)
candidates.one_line: Seventh daughter abandoned at birth for being a girl, who alone journeyed to the western sky for the water of life and became the first shaman and guide of dead souls.

candidates.name: Yeomra Daewang
candidates.one_line: Chief of the Ten Kings of the underworld, judge of the dead — the courtroom boss of the Korean afterlife.

candidates.name: Jowangshin
candidates.one_line: Hearth goddess who lives in the kitchen fire and reports each household's deeds to heaven at year's end.

candidates.name: Seongju
candidates.one_line: Guardian god of the house beam, installed with song when a home is raised — the house itself as patron.

candidates.name: Eopsin
candidates.one_line: Snake goddess of the storeroom whose coiled presence is a family's wealth — if she slithers out, the fortune leaves with her.

candidates.name: Cheuksin
candidates.one_line: Vengeful long-haired maiden goddess of the outhouse who counts her hair strands and strikes those who surprise her.

candidates.name: Jacheongbi
candidates.one_line: Jeju farm goddess who cross-dressed to study among the gods, out-schemed heaven, and brought back the five grains.

candidates.name: Gameunjang-agi
candidates.one_line: Youngest daughter cast out for crediting her own luck over her parents' — proved it by becoming the goddess of fortune itself.

candidates.name: Seolmundae Halmang
candidates.one_line: Giant grandmother who piled up Jeju Island with her skirt-loads of earth, Mt. Halla for a pillow.

candidates.name: Yeongdeung Halmang
candidates.one_line: Visiting grandmother of the winds who sows the year's sea harvest each second lunar month — her welcome-and-farewell rite is UNESCO-listed.

candidates.name: Sonnim
candidates.one_line: The smallpox 'Honored Guests' — traveling plague gods treated with lavish courtesy because offense means a scarred or dead child.

candidates.name: Gangnim Doryeong
candidates.one_line: Mortal enforcer who marched into the underworld and arrested King Yeomra himself, earning the post of death's chief messenger.

candidates.name: Hong Gildong
candidates.one_line: Illegitimate nobleman's son turned magic-wielding outlaw who robs the corrupt and founds his own just kingdom.

candidates.name: Gumiho
candidates.one_line: Nine-tailed fox who wears a beautiful human face and eats livers or hearts — global via K-drama and League of Legends' Ahri.

candidates.name: Imoogi
candidates.one_line: Great serpent that must endure a thousand years — or catch a falling yeouiju — to ascend as a true dragon.

candidates.name: Haetae (Haechi)
candidates.one_line: Horned lion-beast that devours fire and judges the guilty — official mascot of Seoul, stone sentinel of Gyeongbokgung.

candidates.name: Bulgasari
candidates.one_line: Rice-grain homunculus that eats iron and grows into an unkillable colossus — needles to swords to cannon.

candidates.name: Samjogo
candidates.one_line: Three-legged crow of the sun, emblem of Goguryeo — solar power on black wings.

candidates.name: Chollima
candidates.one_line: Winged horse too swift for any rider to mount, covering a thousand li a day.

candidates.name: Dokkaebi
candidates.one_line: Goblin trickster who challenges travelers to ssireum wrestling and dice-like games, punishes the greedy, and conjures gold from its club — a born casino patron.

candidates.name: Gwisin
candidates.one_line: The han-laden dead — white-clad, long-haired virgin ghosts and water ghosts who linger until their grievance is resolved; the engine of all K-horror.

candidates.name: Jeoseung Saja
candidates.one_line: Death's black-hatted civil servant who arrives with a summons scroll and escorts souls to the underworld — a global face via the drama Goblin.

candidates.name: Ungnyeo
candidates.one_line: The bear who endured 100 days of darkness on garlic and mugwort to become a woman, then prayed for and bore Dangun.

candidates.name: Mago Halmi
candidates.one_line: Primordial giant grandmother whose idle earth-moving and urine-torrents carved Korea's mountains and rivers.

candidates.name: Sim Cheong
candidates.one_line: Devoted daughter who sold herself as a sacrifice to the sea for her blind father's sight and rose from the Dragon King's palace as an empress in a lotus.

candidates.name: Dokkaebi bangmangi
candidates.one_line: The goblin's knobbed club: strike it and demand, and gold, food, or whatever is named pours out.

candidates.name: Cheonbuin
candidates.one_line: The three Heavenly Seals Hwanin gave Hwanung as regalia of authority over the human world.

candidates.name: Yeouiju
candidates.one_line: The dragon's wish-fulfilling cinnabar pearl — grasp it and an imoogi ascends; drop it and stay a serpent forever.

candidates.name: Hwansaeng-kkot
candidates.one_line: The resurrection flowers of the western flower fields — laid on bones in the shaman epics, they knit flesh, breath, and soul back in order.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this tradition is formally culturally closed — muism's myths (muga/bonpuli) are published, nationally celebrated, and UNESCO-recognized. D-4 exclusions applied instead: (1) Quasi-historical dynastic founders with legend cycles — Jumong/King Dongmyeong of Goguryeo (egg-born, river-crossing on fish and turtles), Park Hyeokgeose, Seok Talhae, and Kim Alji of Silla, plus Lady Yuhwa and Haemosu as parents in the Jumong cycle — are treated as legend cycles attached to possibly-real persons: story-only material, not entities. (2) Deified real historical humans central to actual muism practice are excluded as entities per D-4 even though they are among the most-worshipped figures in living gut: General Choe Yeong, General Nam I, King Danjong, Guan Yu as Gwanseong Jegun (syncretic Chinese import), and even 20th-century figures (some mudang venerate General MacArthur). This is a real gap the owner should know about: a large share of muism's living pantheon is deified humans and therefore out of scope. (3) The personal momju (patron-spirit) relationships of individual living mudang and the specifics of naerim-gut initiations concern living private individuals and recent dead — not collected. Dangun himself was ruled IN as an entity: mainstream scholarship treats him as mythic (divine-descent narrative, no historical attestation), though the owner should note some nationalist framings treat him as historical.

candidates.name: Coyote
candidates.one_line: Pan-Western trickster-transformer (Navajo, Plains, Plateau, California nations) who steals fire, scatters the stars, and gambles away everything he wins.

candidates.name: Raven
candidates.one_line: Tlingit/Haida trickster-creator who stole the Sun, Moon, and stars from a hoarding chief's boxes and released daylight to the world.

candidates.name: Thunderbird
candidates.one_line: Vast storm-being of Lakota, Anishinaabe, and Pacific Northwest traditions whose wingbeats are thunder and whose eyes flash lightning, eternal enemy of the water monsters.

candidates.name: Wendigo
candidates.one_line: Algonquian (Ojibwe/Cree) winter cannibal spirit of insatiable greed — a serious moral concept in living communities; the antlered horror-movie version is a modern non-Native invention.

candidates.name: Sasquatch (Sasq'ets)
candidates.one_line: Halkomelem/Sts'ailes Coast Salish wild forest giant whose name became the global 'Bigfoot' — household-name cryptid with an Indigenous root.

candidates.name: Sedna
candidates.one_line: Inuit mother of sea mammals, thrown from a boat by her own father, whose severed fingers became the seals and whales that shamans must appease.

candidates.name: Kokopelli
candidates.one_line: Hopi/Pueblo humpbacked flute player of fertility and abundance, ubiquitous in Southwest rock art and (over-)commercialized far beyond his ceremonial home.

candidates.name: Gitche Manitou
candidates.one_line: Anishinaabe/Algonquian Great Spirit and supreme creative mystery, popularly rendered in English as the 'Great Spirit'.

candidates.name: Wakan Tanka
candidates.one_line: Lakota 'Great Mystery' — the sacred totality addressed in living Lakota ceremony rather than a single anthropomorphic god.

candidates.name: White Buffalo Calf Woman
candidates.one_line: Lakota holy woman-messenger who brought the sacred pipe and the seven rites; central to living Lakota religion (the physical pipe itself is closed material).

candidates.name: Nanabozho
candidates.one_line: Ojibwe/Anishinaabe great hare culture-hero and trickster who co-created the world after the flood and named the plants and animals.

candidates.name: Glooscap
candidates.one_line: Wabanaki/Mi'kmaq giant culture-hero and transformer who tamed the winds, shrank the animals to their present size, and promised to return.

candidates.name: Iktomi
candidates.one_line: Lakota spider trickster, shapeshifting schemer whose plans always backfire — teaching by bad example in living Lakota storytelling.

candidates.name: Spider Grandmother
candidates.one_line: Hopi/Pueblo (and Navajo Spider Woman) creatrix and helper who taught weaving and guides heroes through the underworlds.

candidates.name: Changing Woman (Asdzaa Nadleehe)
candidates.one_line: Navajo (Dine) holy person who ages and renews with the seasons, mother of the Hero Twins, honored in the living Kinaalda ceremony.

candidates.name: Monster Slayer & Born-for-Water
candidates.one_line: Navajo (Dine) Hero Twins who journeyed to their father the Sun and cleansed the world of monsters — core of living ceremonial narrative.

candidates.name: Sky Woman
candidates.one_line: Haudenosaunee ancestress who fell from the Sky World onto Turtle's back, where earth piled on the shell became the world (Turtle Island).

candidates.name: Noqoilpi, the Great Gambler
candidates.one_line: Navajo (Dine) gambling god of Chaco Canyon who won a people's goods, wives, and bodies at games of chance until the gods rigged a rematch and shot him into the sky — lost everything, vowing return.

candidates.name: Skinwalker (yee naaldlooshii)
candidates.one_line: Navajo (Dine) shape-shifting witch; heavily pop-culturized (Skinwalker Ranch) but a living taboo subject Navajo people traditionally avoid discussing.

candidates.name: Deer Woman
candidates.one_line: Plains and Woodlands (Lakota, Ojibwe, Ponca and others) beautiful woman with deer's hooves who lures predatory men to their doom; revived in Reservation Dogs and American Gods.

candidates.name: Mishipeshu (Underwater Panther)
candidates.one_line: Ojibwe/Anishinaabe horned copper-guarding water lynx of Lake Superior, adversary of the Thunderbirds, painted at Agawa Rock.

candidates.name: Uktena
candidates.one_line: Cherokee horned serpent with a blazing diamond in its forehead; to look upon it dazzles, to slay it takes a marked arrow and perfect nerve (Mooney 1900).

candidates.name: Ulun'suti
candidates.one_line: Cherokee divining crystal cut from the Uktena's forehead — grants prophecy but demands blood, kept hidden and fed by its keeper (Mooney 1900).

candidates.name: Box of Daylight
candidates.one_line: Tlingit nested cedar boxes in which a great chief hoarded the stars, Moon, and Sun until Raven tricked his way in and freed them (Swanton 1909).

candidates.name: Pukwudgie
candidates.one_line: Wampanoag/Wabanaki forest little-people, mischievous to dangerous, boosted to games/fantasy audiences by Harry Potter's Ilvermorny houses.

candidates.name: Dzunukwa
candidates.one_line: Kwakwaka'wakw wild woman of the woods who carries off children in her basket; her mask and story are owned clan prerogatives, not free folklore.

candidates.name: Sisiutl
candidates.one_line: Kwakwaka'wakw double-headed sea serpent whose gaze turns enemies to stone; a crest being owned by specific families.

candidates.name: Amarok
candidates.one_line: Inuit gigantic lone wolf that hunts the foolish hunter who goes out alone at night.

candidates.name: Qalupalik
candidates.one_line: Inuit green-skinned sea hag who hums beneath the ice and snatches wandering children into her amautik pouch.

candidates.name: Kushtaka
candidates.one_line: Tlingit land-otter men who lure the drowning and lost, transforming them into their own kind; clan-narrative material in a living culture.

candidates.name: Flying Head
candidates.one_line: Haudenosaunee ravenous disembodied giant head with fiery eyes and lank wings, defeated when a woman tricked it into swallowing hot stones.

candidates.name: Stone Coat (Genoskwa)
candidates.one_line: Haudenosaunee cannibal giant armored in stone skin, killable only through its vulnerable spots; surfaced for TV audiences via Grimm.

candidates.name: Selu, the Corn Mother
candidates.one_line: Cherokee First Woman whose body, dragged over the earth by her sons, gave humanity corn (Mooney 1900).

closed_material_notes: Substantial closed material exists and was deliberately NOT collected.
