(1) Hopi esoteric katsina/ceremonial knowledge: Hopi Tribal Council Resolution H-72-94 (1994) formally restricts records depicting esoteric ritual/ceremonial/religious knowledge as Hopi cultural property — no katsinam are listed as entities; only the already fully-public Kokopelli figure is included, flagged restricted+living. (2) Zuni esoteric religion (kiva societies, Ahayu:da war gods — actively repatriated sacred objects) — excluded entirely. (3) Navajo ceremonial chantway content (Blessingway/Nightway details, sandpainting specifics) is practitioner-held; only the widely published Matthews-era narrative frame is used, and skinwalker material carries a living discussion taboo. (4) The White Buffalo Calf Pipe is a real, extant sacred bundle with a living lineage keeper (Looking Horse family) — the figure is listed but the physical pipe is NOT offered as an artifact seed. (5) Lakota Sun Dance and pipe ceremony specifics: closed per the 1993 Declaration of War Against Exploiters of Lakota Spirituality. (6) Ojibwe Midewiwin (Grand Medicine Society) teachings and birch-bark scrolls are initiate-only — excluded. (7) Tlingit/Haida/Kwakwaka'wakw stories and crest beings are clan-owned property (at.oow/potlatch prerogatives); only broadly republished PD-era narratives are listed, flagged restricted where clan ownership is strongest. (8) Wendigo: listed, but with an active appropriation debate — Algonquian communities object to the decultured antlered-monster treatment; owner review advised before any depiction. D-4 exclusions (real historical humans whose legend cycles are story-only material): Hiawatha and the Great Peacemaker Deganawida (Haudenosaunee — note the Peacemaker's name itself is traditionally spoken only in ceremony), Handsome Lake (Seneca prophet), Sweet Medicine (Cheyenne prophet), and Popé (Pueblo Revolt leader).

candidates.name: Perun
candidates.one_line: Oak-and-axe thunderer atop Vladimir's Kyiv pantheon, sworn on by treaty-oaths and toppled into the Dnieper at Christianization.

candidates.name: Veles
candidates.one_line: Horned god of cattle, wealth, magic and the watery underworld, cyclically struck down by Perun's lightning only to rise again.

candidates.name: Svarog
candidates.one_line: Celestial smith-father who forged the sun and dropped the first plough and battle-axe from the sky.

candidates.name: Dazhbog
candidates.one_line: Sun-giver and 'grandfather of the Rus', dispensing wealth and daylight from his sky-hall.

candidates.name: Mokosh
candidates.one_line: The one goddess of Vladimir's pantheon — moist Mother Earth who spins the threads of fate and women's work.

candidates.name: Stribog
candidates.one_line: Grandfather of the winds, whose grandchildren-gales shoot arrows across the steppe in the Igor Tale.

candidates.name: Khors
candidates.one_line: Enigmatic solar deity of the Kyiv pantheon, possibly Iranian-borrowed, whom Vseslav the sorcerer-prince outraced by night.

candidates.name: Simargl
candidates.one_line: Winged-dog guardian of seeds and shoots, a griffin-like oddity in the 980 idol row.

candidates.name: Svetovit
candidates.one_line: Four-faced war-oracle of Arkona whose sacred white horse's steps decided whether campaigns would prosper or fail.

candidates.name: Triglav
candidates.one_line: Three-headed god of Szczecin whose gold-blindfolded heads survey sky, earth and underworld at once.

candidates.name: Radegast
candidates.one_line: Host-god of the Redarian temple-fortress, greeting guests with a bird on his helm — his name now sells Czech beer.

candidates.name: Chernobog
candidates.one_line: The 'Black God' of ill fortune, cursed over the drinking-bowl in Helmold — and unforgettable as Fantasia's bald-mountain demon.

candidates.name: Belobog
candidates.one_line: The bright 'White God' of good fortune, Chernobog's inferred twin — historicity contested, duality irresistible.

candidates.name: Rod
candidates.one_line: Primordial begetter of kin and fate, honored with the Rozhanitsy birth-mothers at every family feast.

candidates.name: Marzanna
candidates.one_line: Winter-death goddess whose straw effigy is still drowned and burned every spring — a deity ritually unmade annually.

candidates.name: Jarilo
candidates.one_line: Barefoot spring-fertility youth who rides in on a white horse, weds, is dismembered, and dies each autumn to rise again.

candidates.name: Lada
candidates.one_line: Purported goddess of love and harmony from wedding-song refrains — beloved of Rodnovery, doubted by philologists.

candidates.name: Zorya
candidates.one_line: The Auroras — dawn and dusk sisters who open and shut the sun's gates and keep the doomsday hound chained.

candidates.name: Baba Yaga
candidates.one_line: Iron-toothed crone flying in a mortar, dwelling in a chicken-legged hut ringed with glowing skulls — helper or devourer by whim.

candidates.name: Vasilisa the Beautiful
candidates.one_line: Orphan armed with her dead mother's blessing-doll who out-serves Baba Yaga and carries home fire in a skull lantern.

candidates.name: Koschei the Deathless
candidates.one_line: Skeletal abductor-sorcerer who hid his death in a needle, in an egg, in a duck, in a hare, in an iron chest — existence itself his wager.

candidates.name: Upyr
candidates.one_line: The original Slavic vampire — a bloated revenant that predates and shaped the Western Dracula archetype.

candidates.name: Strzyga
candidates.one_line: Two-souled, two-hearted demoness who rises from the grave to shriek and feed — famously cursed royalty in The Witcher.

candidates.name: Zmey Gorynych
candidates.one_line: Three-headed fire-breathing dragon of the byliny, blocking rivers and bridges until a bogatyr cuts him down.

candidates.name: Firebird
candidates.one_line: Blazing golden bird whose single dropped feather lights a room and dooms whoever picks it up to a quest.

candidates.name: Sirin
candidates.one_line: Woman-faced bird of paradise whose sorrowful song lures listeners to forget the world and die.

candidates.name: Domovoi
candidates.one_line: Whiskered house-grandfather behind the stove who guards the household — or wrecks it when disrespected.

candidates.name: Leshy
candidates.one_line: Shape-shifting forest master, tall as the trees or small as grass, who leads travelers in circles until appeased.

candidates.name: Rusalka
candidates.one_line: Drowned-maiden water spirit combing her hair on the riverbank, singing men down to the depths.

candidates.name: Vodyanoy
candidates.one_line: Frog-faced old man of the millpond who drags the unwary under and keeps drowned souls in teacups.

candidates.name: Kikimora
candidates.one_line: Night-hag of the threshold who tangles spinning, rattles dishes and rides sleepers' chests.

candidates.name: Vila
candidates.one_line: South Slavic mountain nymph, storm-dancing and battle-fierce, who may bless a hero as a blood-sister or shoot him down.

candidates.name: Likho
candidates.one_line: One-eyed embodiment of evil fortune — seek it out and bad luck rides home on your shoulders forever.

candidates.name: Dola
candidates.one_line: Personal fate-and-fortune spirit assigned at birth; her dark twin Nedolya deals the losing hands.

candidates.name: Ilya Muromets
candidates.one_line: Paralyzed peasant's son healed at thirty-three who becomes the mightiest bogatyr and fells Nightingale the Robber.

candidates.name: Dobrynya Nikitich
candidates.one_line: Courtly dragon-slaying bogatyr who battles Zmey Gorynych for three days and frees the captives of Sorochinsk.

candidates.name: Sadko
candidates.one_line: Novgorod minstrel who wins a wager against the merchant guilds with the Sea Tsar's secret help — then must play at the bottom of the sea.

candidates.name: Ivan Tsarevich
candidates.one_line: The archetypal youngest-prince quester of Russian fairy tales, forever chasing Firebirds and rescuing brides from Koschei.

candidates.name: Sword Kladenets
candidates.one_line: The self-swinging 'hidden blade' of Russian tales, dug from a barrow or drawn from under a giant head, that fights by itself.

candidates.name: Alatyr
candidates.one_line: The white father-of-all-stones on the isle of Buyan, from beneath which all rivers of healing flow.

closed_material_notes: Nothing in the Slavic corpus is culturally closed or restricted — it is fully public-sphere published material. Exclusions and cautions instead: (1) D-4 exclusions — Prince Vladimir 'Fair Sun' of the bylina cycles, and the plausible historical prototypes behind Ilya Muromets (the monk Ilya Pechersky) and Dobrynya Nikitich (Vladimir's historical uncle Dobrynya), are real humans; the bogatyrs are listed as legend-figures only and their historical identifications are story-only material. (2) The 'Book of Veles' is a 20th-century forgery treated as scripture by some Rodnover groups — excluded as a source; any content derived from it should be rejected in later waves. (3) Belobog and Lada carry contested-historicity caveats (scholarly reconstructions/back-formations rather than securely attested cult) — kept on the list for their game value but later waves should not fabricate 'attested' myths for them. (4) Rodnovery's political entanglements (some nationalist currents) are a depiction-framing caution for the 'living'-flagged gods, not a closure.

candidates.name: Bondye
candidates.one_line: The distant supreme creator behind all the lwa — never served directly, never possessed, never seen at any table.

candidates.name: Papa Legba
candidates.one_line: Limping old gatekeeper of the crossroads who must open the gate before any other spirit can be reached.

candidates.name: Danbala Wèdo
candidates.one_line: The great white serpent of primordial creation, oldest and purest of the Rada, who speaks only in hisses.

candidates.name: Ayida Wèdo
candidates.one_line: The rainbow serpent arched over the waters, Danbala's consort and co-holder of the sky.

candidates.name: Ezili Freda
candidates.one_line: Rada lady of love, luxury, and perfume whose visits always end in tears — no offering is ever enough.

candidates.name: Ezili Dantò
candidates.one_line: Scar-cheeked Petwo mother with a dagger, fierce single-parent protector tied by tradition to the revolution's opening ceremony at Bwa Kayiman.

candidates.name: Ogou Feray
candidates.one_line: Warrior lwa of iron, fire, and revolution who drinks rum and demands the machete be fed.

candidates.name: Agwe
candidates.one_line: Admiral of the ocean whose offerings are set adrift on rafts and must sink to be accepted.

candidates.name: La Sirèn
candidates.one_line: Mermaid queen of the sea whose trumpet-song can pull the unwary underwater for years of hidden schooling.

candidates.name: Baron Samedi
candidates.one_line: Top-hatted, rum-soaked, obscene lord of the grave — no one enters death without his say-so, and he digs every grave.

candidates.name: Maman Brigitte
candidates.one_line: Foul-mouthed queen of cemeteries and wife of the Baron, guardian of the first woman's grave in any burial ground.

candidates.name: Gede Nibo
candidates.one_line: Dandyish psychopomp of those who die young, dressed in purple, dispenser of lewd jokes and winning lottery numbers.

candidates.name: Baron Kriminèl
candidates.one_line: The feared executioner among the Gede, invoked for swift and terrible justice against the guilty.

candidates.name: Kouzen Azaka
candidates.one_line: Straw-hatted peasant farmer lwa with a shoulder sack, patron of agriculture who haggles over every offering.

candidates.name: Loko
candidates.one_line: Spirit of trees, healing leaves, and the priesthood itself — guardian of temples and legitimacy.

candidates.name: Ayizan
candidates.one_line: The first mambo, veiled in shredded palm fronds, patron of the marketplace and of initiation's threshold.

candidates.name: Simbi
candidates.one_line: Serpentine lwa of fresh water, magic, and communication who ferries messages between the worlds.

candidates.name: Bossou
candidates.one_line: Three-horned bull lwa of raw strength, a fierce protector with roots in Dahomean royal cult.

candidates.name: Marinèt
candidates.one_line: Volatile Petwo mistress of screech owls and flame, served apart and with great caution even by her own.

candidates.name: Ti Jean Petwo
candidates.one_line: Fiery one-legged Petwo trickster who dances in flame and climbs pinnacles of fire.

candidates.name: Marasa
candidates.one_line: The sacred divine twins, childlike and powerful, honored first among the dead and doubly demanding of sweets.

candidates.name: Zonbi
candidates.one_line: The soulless revenant raised and enslaved by a bòkò — the original zombie, a horror of stolen will, not hunger.

candidates.name: Lougawou
candidates.one_line: Skin-shedding night-witch who flies as a ball of fire and preys on the blood of children.

candidates.name: Baka
candidates.one_line: A malevolent little spirit-servant in animal form, bought at a terrible price to guard property or attack rivals.

candidates.name: Djab
candidates.one_line: A wild, unaffiliated 'devil' spirit contracted outside the regular rites for fast, dangerous personal work.

closed_material_notes: CLOSED / NOT COLLECTED: (1) Initiation-level material — the kanzo grades, the asson (sacred rattle of priesthood), pwen (spirit-points), the esoteric layers of priyè Ginen and ritual songs, and accurate vèvè construction — is restricted to initiates per the tradition itself (Vodou is a graded initiatory religion; secrecy also has roots in survival under slavery-era persecution). None of it is collected, and the game should never depict ritual procedure as-if-accurate. (2) The Bizango/Sanpwèl secret societies are fully closed institutions; excluded entirely. (3) Ritual objects — asson, govi (spirit jars), pakèt kongo, drapo Vodou (sequined flags), vèvè — are living ritual technology of a practiced religion, not mythological loot; the artifact class is deliberately left at zero. (4) D-4 exclusions (real historical humans; legend cycles usable as story-only material): François Makandal, Dutty Boukman, and Cécile Fatiman (revolution figures wrapped in Vodou legend); Jean-Jacques Dessalines, whose partial deification survives as the lwa aspect 'Ogou Desalin' — that absorption may be referenced as lore but Dessalines himself is not an entity; Marie Laveau of the related New Orleans Voodoo tradition; and François 'Papa Doc' Duvalier's deliberate political appropriation of Baron Samedi's iconography — a documented cautionary tale about costume-deep misuse of this exact imagery. (5) Related traditions (Louisiana Voodoo, Dominican Vudú, and the 21 Divisions) are distinct living practices only lightly touched here; if the owner wants them, they warrant their own sensitivity review rather than being folded into Haitian Vodou.

candidates.name: Ahura Mazda (Ohrmazd)
candidates.one_line: The Wise Lord, all-good creator locked in a fixed-term 9,000-year cosmic contest with the Destructive Spirit — the original high-stakes house player.

candidates.name: Angra Mainyu (Ahriman)
candidates.one_line: The Destructive Spirit who chose the lie, corrupts all creation, and is destined to be utterly unmade at the renovation of the world.

candidates.name: Mithra (Mihr)
candidates.one_line: Thousand-eared, ten-thousand-eyed yazata of covenant and contract who punishes oath-breakers — the ultimate enforcer of a wager's terms.

candidates.name: Anahita (Aredvi Sura Anahita)
candidates.one_line: Radiant goddess of the world-river, fertility, and victory, to whom heroes and tyrants alike sacrificed for boons.

candidates.name: Verethragna (Bahram)
candidates.one_line: Yazata of smashing victory who manifests in ten shapes — wind, bull, stallion, camel, boar, raven, ram, warrior and more.

candidates.name: Sraosha (Sorush)
candidates.one_line: Yazata of obedience and the divine word who guards souls through the night and escorts the dead to judgment.

candidates.name: Rashnu
candidates.one_line: The incorruptible judge whose golden scales weigh every soul's deeds at the Chinvat Bridge — the house's honest oddsmaker.

candidates.name: Atar
candidates.one_line: Yazata of holy fire, son of Ahura Mazda, who wrestled the dragon Azi Dahaka for the divine glory.

candidates.name: Tishtrya (Tir)
candidates.one_line: The star Sirius as a white stallion, who battles the drought demon Apaosha each year for the world's rain — a recurring divine rematch.

candidates.name: Haoma
candidates.one_line: Deified sacred plant and priest of the gods, whose pressed golden elixir grants strength, healing, and insight.

candidates.name: Vayu-Vata
candidates.one_line: Double-natured wind yazata who blows both blessing and death — the god both sides of the cosmic war must pass through.

candidates.name: Zurvan
candidates.one_line: Infinite Time of the heterodox Zurvanite myth, who sacrificed a thousand years gambling on a perfect son — and whose one doubt birthed Ahriman.

candidates.name: Amesha Spentas (the six Bounteous Immortals)
candidates.one_line: Collective of six divine emanations — Good Mind, Best Truth, Desirable Dominion, Holy Devotion, Wholeness, Immortality — each guarding one creation.

candidates.name: Ashi (Ashi Vanghuhi)
candidates.one_line: Yazata of fortune, reward, and recompense — good luck personified as a radiant maiden who rides with the righteous; casino nobility.

candidates.name: Nairyosangha
candidates.one_line: The fire-natured herald who carries Ahura Mazda's messages between heaven and earth.

candidates.name: Aeshma
candidates.one_line: Demon of wrath with the bloody mace, ancestor of the biblical Asmodeus.

candidates.name: Apaosha
candidates.one_line: Drought demon in the shape of a hairless black horse, Tishtrya's eternal opponent in the battle for rain.

candidates.name: Astovidatu
candidates.one_line: The bone-divider, demon of death who casts the noose no mortal escapes.

candidates.name: Jahi
candidates.one_line: The demoness whose taunt roused Ahriman from his 3,000-year stupor to assault creation.

candidates.name: Aka Manah
candidates.one_line: Evil Mind, arch-counterpart of Good Mind, who whispers corrupt intention into every choice.

candidates.name: Druj Nasu
candidates.one_line: Corpse-demoness who flies as a fly from the north to seize the dead — the reason for the tradition's elaborate purity laws.

candidates.name: Bushyasta
candidates.one_line: The long-handed yellow demoness of sloth who lulls the world back to sleep at dawn.

candidates.name: Azi Dahaka (Zahhak)
candidates.one_line: Three-headed, six-eyed dragon-tyrant with serpents growing from his shoulders, chained under Mount Damavand until the end of days — then slain for good.

candidates.name: Simurgh (Saena)
candidates.one_line: The colossal benevolent bird whose wingbeats scatter the seeds of the Tree of All Remedies.

candidates.name: Gandarewa
candidates.one_line: Yellow-heeled water monster of the cosmic sea, dragged out and slain by the hero Keresaspa after a nine-day battle.

candidates.name: Gavaevodata
candidates.one_line: The uniquely-created primordial ox, first animal of creation, whose death seeded the world with all beneficial beasts and plants.

candidates.name: Yima (Jamshid)
candidates.one_line: Golden-age first king who built the Vara to save creation from apocalyptic winter — then claimed divinity, lost his divine glory, and fell; the archetypal bankrupt sovereign.

candidates.name: Thraetaona (Fereydun)
candidates.one_line: Dragon-slaying hero-king who overthrew Azi Dahaka and bound him beneath Damavand rather than kill him.

candidates.name: Keresaspa (Garshasp)
candidates.one_line: Club-wielding monster-slayer who once cooked dinner on a sleeping dragon's back — destined to wake at the end of time to finish Azi Dahaka.

candidates.name: Rostam
candidates.one_line: The Shahnameh's mightiest champion, tamer of the demon-horse Rakhsh, slayer of the White Div — and unknowing killer of his own son.

candidates.name: Kay Khosrow
candidates.one_line: The just Kayanian king who ended the great war, then renounced his throne and vanished occulted into the mountains, awaiting the renovation.

candidates.name: Fravashi
candidates.one_line: Pre-existent guardian spirits of all souls, honored at the year's-end Fravardigan festival — the winged Faravahar emblem is theirs.

candidates.name: Daena
candidates.one_line: The embodiment of one's own deeds who meets the soul at the Chinvat Bridge — a beautiful maiden or a foul hag, depending on the life you played.

candidates.name: Khvarenah (Farr)
candidates.one_line: The divine royal fortune-glory that can be won, held, or catastrophically lost — the literal currency of legitimate power; it fled Yima as a bird.

candidates.name: Pairika (Peri)
candidates.one_line: Seductive sorceress-spirits of the Avesta who dim the stars, later softened into the beautiful peris of Persian folklore.

candidates.name: Cup of Jamshid (Jam-e Jam)
candidates.one_line: The world-revealing cup in which the seven heavens and all events on earth can be observed — perfect surveillance for a casino floor.

candidates.name: Vara of Yima
candidates.one_line: The underground ark-fortress built to preserve the best seeds of every living thing through the world-ending winter.

closed_material_notes: D-4 exclusions: Zarathushtra (Zoroaster) himself is a historical human prophet and is excluded as an entity; his legend cycle (miraculous birth laughing, temptation by Angra Mainyu, conversion of King Vishtaspa) is story-only material. Vishtaspa and other possibly-historical Kayanian-dynasty figures at the legend/history boundary were included only where clearly mythic (Kay Khosrow's occultation); later Sasanian-era figures are excluded. Nothing in the textual corpus is culturally closed — the Avesta and Pahlavi books are fully published in public-domain translation. However, living-community restrictions exist on PRACTICE, not texts: fire temple inner sanctums and the Yasna ceremony are closed to non-Zoroastrians, Parsi orthodoxy restricts entry to the community itself (no conversion in), and funerary dakhma (Tower of Silence) practice is a sensitive living matter — none of this blocks the census, but depicting fire-temple interiors, the sacred fires (e.g. Iranshah Atash Behram), or funerary rites on-screen should be owner-gated. Depiction of Ahura Mazda and the prophet in a gambling frame is the tradition's highest sensitivity point: God as literally all-good and non-capricious is core theology, so an Ahura Mazda who gambles misrepresents living doctrine — recommend off-screen-investor framing per the living gate.

candidates.name: Rainbow Serpent
candidates.one_line: The immense creator-serpent who carved rivers and waterholes into the land and can unmake them in flood when the law is broken.

candidates.name: Baiame
candidates.one_line: Sky-father and law-giver of the Gamilaraay and Wiradjuri who made the land, gave the first laws, and withdrew to the sky country.

candidates.name: Bunjil
candidates.one_line: The wedge-tailed eagle creator of the Kulin nations, who shaped people from bark and clay and watches the land as protector.

candidates.name: Daramulum
candidates.one_line: One-legged sky hero, son of Baiame, whose voice is the bullroarer's growl at initiation grounds.

candidates.name: Wagyl
candidates.one_line: The Noongar rainbow serpent whose body dug the Swan River — still invoked today in disputes over building on Perth's sacred sites.

candidates.name: Eingana
candidates.one_line: Snake-mother of creation who holds a sinew tied to every living thing — if she lets go, that life ends.

candidates.name: Gnowee
candidates.one_line: Solar woman who climbs the sky each day with a torch, forever searching for her lost son.

candidates.name: Wurrunna
candidates.one_line: Gamilaraay culture-hero and wanderer whose journeys and transgressions explain features of the land (published in Langloh Parker's tales).

candidates.name: Bunyip
candidates.one_line: The inconsistent horror of still dark water — dog-faced, tusked, flippered, seal-like — that bellows from billabongs and takes those who swim at night.

candidates.name: Yowie
candidates.one_line: The hairy giant of the eastern ranges, Australia's bigfoot, walking out of Aboriginal tradition into modern cryptid fame.

candidates.name: Tiddalik
candidates.one_line: The greedy frog who drank every drop of water in the world and had to be made to laugh to give it back.

candidates.name: Muldjewangk
candidates.one_line: Monstrous being of the Murray River that drags down children and once blistered a steamboat captain to death for firing on it.

candidates.name: Yara-ma-yha-who
candidates.one_line: Little red frog-mouthed man who drops from fig trees, drains victims with sucker-tipped fingers, and swallows them whole — each regurgitation leaves you shorter and redder.

candidates.name: Papinijuwari
candidates.one_line: One-eyed sky giants of Tiwi tradition who scent the blood of the sick and dying and come to drink it.

candidates.name: Mimi
candidates.one_line: Stick-thin rock spirits of Arnhem Land, so frail wind would snap them, who taught the first people to hunt, paint, and dance.

candidates.name: Quinkan
candidates.one_line: Spindly and squat spirit-beings of Cape York, some tricksters and some predators, painted for millennia in the Laura rock-art galleries.

candidates.name: Dirawong
candidates.one_line: The goanna guardian of the Bundjalung who battled the Rainbow Serpent to protect the people, their combat carved into the coastline.

candidates.name: Min Min lights
candidates.one_line: Bobbing ghost-lights of the outback that follow travellers at a fixed distance — folklore says whoever catches one never returns.

closed_material_notes: Substantial material was deliberately NOT collected because the traditions themselves restrict it: (1) WANDJINA (Worrorra/Ngarinyin/Wunambal, Kimberley) — supreme cloud-ancestor spirits whose depiction is restricted to initiated artists of the custodial clans; unauthorized reproduction is treated as spiritually harmful and has been the subject of formal cultural-heritage action (WIPO-documented). Excluded entirely despite being visually famous. (2) SECRET-SACRED OBJECTS — tjurunga/churinga (Arrernte and Central Desert) are initiation-restricted objects whose viewing by the uninitiated is prohibited; this is why the artifact class returns ZERO candidates — the tradition's mythic 'artifacts' are precisely its closed material. (3) YOLNGU CEREMONIAL CYCLES — the Djang'kawu and Wawalag sisters cycles and the Yurlungur/Julunggul rainbow-serpent material were published in mid-century ethnography (Warner, the Berndts) but are ceremony-linked, partly gender-restricted, and now handled under AIATSIS repatriation/access protocols; listed nowhere above. (4) SEVEN SISTERS SONGLINES — the general Pleiades-pursuit outline is public (national museum exhibitions), but long segments are men's- or women's-restricted knowledge; excluded as an entity. (5) INITIATION LORE around Baiame/Daramulum (bora-ground content) is men's business; only the openly published sky-father outline is listed. (6) Gender-restricted song cycles generally: much Central and Western Desert material is clan-owned and never validly publishable. (7) D-4 exclusions: 'Captain Cook' legend cycles (widespread post-contact narrative complexes about a real human) are story-only material; kurdaitcha (ritual executioners) are real human roles, not entities. (8) Naming protocols: some communities restrict speaking names of the dead and of certain beings; any in-game use of listed names should go through owner review — hence the blanket 'restricted' flag on every candidate. Finally, an honest absence: no luck/gambling deity and no god-wagering myth exists in the published corpus, so this tradition contributes no casino-nobility candidates.

candidates.name: Cthulhu
candidates.one_line: Dead-but-dreaming octopus-dragon priest of the Old Ones in sunken R'lyeh, whose stirring bends the dreams of the world ('The Call of Cthulhu', pub. 1928, PD as of 2026).

candidates.name: Nyarlathotep
candidates.one_line: The Crawling Chaos, thousand-formed messenger and soul of the Outer Gods, the only one who walks among humans and enjoys it (prose-poem 1920, solidly PD).

candidates.name: Azathoth
candidates.one_line: The blind idiot god, nuclear chaos gnawing at the center of infinity, lulled by drums and flutes lest reality end (named from 1922; PD).

candidates.name: Yog-Sothoth
candidates.one_line: The Gate and the Key, coterminous with all time and space, iridescent spheres that father monsters on mortal women ('The Dunwich Horror', pub. 1929, PD).

candidates.name: Shub-Niggurath
candidates.one_line: The Black Goat of the Woods with a Thousand Young, invoked in rituals across the corpus but never seen on the page (invocations from 1928 on; PD).

candidates.name: Dagon
candidates.one_line: Colossal fish-god of the Deep Ones, Lovecraft's 1919 borrowing of the Philistine deity for undersea horror ('Dagon' 1919; PD; syncretic with the Semitic Dagon).

candidates.name: Hastur
candidates.one_line: The Unspeakable One of Carcosa, grown from Bierce's benign shepherd-god (1893) through Chambers to a mythos Great Old One — fully PD at the root.

candidates.name: Nodens
candidates.one_line: Grey Lord of the Great Abyss riding a shell drawn by night-gaunts, rare benevolent elder power (from 'The Strange High House in the Mist', written 1926; syncretic with the Romano-British god Nodens).

candidates.name: Yig
candidates.one_line: Father of Serpents of the American plains, half-human snake-god whose curse replaces children with serpent-things ('The Curse of Yig', Lovecraft/Bishop, pub. 1929, PD).

candidates.name: Tsathoggua
candidates.one_line: Sleepy toad-bat god of lightless N'kai — Clark Ashton Smith's creation (1929–31); CAS estate/CASiana Literary Enterprises asserts claims on Smith's corpus.

candidates.name: Cthugha
candidates.one_line: Living-flame Great Old One imprisoned near Fomalhaut — August Derleth's 1944 elemental addition; Derleth estate/Arkham House claimant.

candidates.name: Ithaqua
candidates.one_line: The Wind-Walker of the arctic wastes — Derleth's Great Old One (claimant: Derleth estate/Arkham House), built on Algernon Blackwood's PD 'The Wendigo' (1910).

candidates.name: The King in Yellow
candidates.one_line: Tattered-robed phantom and the sanity-destroying play that bears his name, from Chambers' 1895 collection — fully PD and a True Detective revival darling.

candidates.name: Mother Hydra
candidates.one_line: Dagon's monstrous consort, matriarch of the Deep Ones ('The Shadow Over Innsmouth', pub. 1936 — Lovecraft-solo, unrenewed, treated PD; year flagged for review).

candidates.name: Shoggoth
candidates.one_line: Protoplasmic slave-things of the Elder Things that learned to imitate their masters — 'Tekeli-li!' ('At the Mountains of Madness', pub. 1936, treated PD; now also an AI metaphor).

candidates.name: Deep Ones
candidates.one_line: Immortal fish-frog people who trade gold and hybrid children with coastal towns ('The Shadow Over Innsmouth', pub. 1936, treated PD).

candidates.name: Mi-Go
candidates.one_line: Fungoid crustacean miners from Yuggoth who carry human brains to the stars in cylinders ('The Whisperer in Darkness', pub. 1931, treated PD).

candidates.name: Elder Things
candidates.one_line: Barrel-bodied starfish-headed scientists who built Antarctic cities and bred the shoggoths ('At the Mountains of Madness', pub. 1936, treated PD).

candidates.name: Night-gaunts
candidates.one_line: Faceless rubbery black fliers that tickle dreamers into the abyss, out of Lovecraft's own childhood nightmares (Fungi from Yuggoth sonnets, 1929–30, PD).

candidates.name: Hounds of Tindalos
candidates.one_line: Lean angular hunters that enter our world through corners of time — Frank Belknap Long, Weird Tales 1929; unrenewed and generally treated PD.

candidates.name: Ghouls
candidates.one_line: Rubbery canine-faced corpse-eaters of tunnels beneath graveyards, painted from life by Pickman ('Pickman's Model', 1927, PD).

candidates.name: Byakhee
candidates.one_line: Interstellar steeds summoned with a whistle and space-mead to serve Hastur — named by Derleth (1944); Derleth estate/Arkham House claimant.

candidates.name: The Colour Out of Space
candidates.one_line: An unnameable hue from a meteorite that drinks the life of a farm and its family (1927, PD; 2019 Nicolas Cage film boosted recognition).

candidates.name: Brown Jenkin
candidates.one_line: Rat-thing familiar with a bearded human face, courier between the witch Keziah and Nyarlathotep ('The Dreams in the Witch House', pub. 1933, treated PD).

candidates.name: Randolph Carter
candidates.one_line: Boston dreamer-occultist who quested through the Dreamlands to unknown Kadath and back — the corpus's nearest thing to a legendary hero (stories 1919–1927, PD).

candidates.name: Abdul Alhazred
candidates.one_line: The fictional 'Mad Arab' of Sanaa, author of the Necronomicon, devoured by an invisible thing in daylight (named 1921/1927, PD; handle with care as an Orientalist caricature).

candidates.name: Herbert West
candidates.one_line: Icy Miskatonic medical prodigy who reanimates ever-fresher corpses ('Herbert West—Reanimator', 1922, PD; Re-Animator films carry the recognition).

candidates.name: Joseph Curwen
candidates.one_line: Undying Salem alchemist who farms the 'essential saltes' of the dead for their secrets (The Case of Charles Dexter Ward, written 1927, pub. 1941 unrenewed — treated PD, year flagged).

candidates.name: Keziah Mason
candidates.one_line: Salem witch who escaped her cell through hyperspace geometry and still takes children for the Black Man ('The Dreams in the Witch House', pub. 1933, treated PD).

candidates.name: Wilbur Whateley
candidates.one_line: Goatish nine-foot son of Yog-Sothoth raised to open the gates over Dunwich — and his worse invisible twin ('The Dunwich Horror', pub. 1929, PD).

candidates.name: Cats of Ulthar
candidates.one_line: In Ulthar no man may kill a cat — the town learned why in one night ('The Cats of Ulthar', 1920, PD); ready-made event/ally material.

candidates.name: Zoogs
candidates.one_line: Small furtive brown dwellers of the Enchanted Wood of the Dreamlands, curious, gossipy, and occasionally man-eating (Dream-Quest, written 1927, pub. 1943 unrenewed — treated PD).

candidates.name: Necronomicon
candidates.one_line: The dread grimoire of Alhazred, locked away at Miskatonic — the most famous fictional book on Earth ('History of the Necronomicon' 1927, PD).

candidates.name: Yellow Sign
candidates.one_line: A sigil that marks its viewer for the King in Yellow (Chambers 1895, fully PD).

candidates.name: Shining Trapezohedron
candidates.one_line: A crazily-angled crystal window on all time and space that summons the Haunter of the Dark when gazed into ('The Haunter of the Dark', pub. 1936, treated PD).

candidates.name: Silver Key
candidates.one_line: Arabesque-carved key that opens the gate back into dream and lost time ('The Silver Key', pub. 1929, PD).

candidates.name: Pnakotic Manuscripts
candidates.one_line: Pre-human fragmentary chronicles handed down from the Great Race, oldest of the mythos tomes (first named 1918, PD).

closed_material_notes: Nothing in this family is culturally closed — it is a literary corpus with no living religious community; the ironic/chaos-magick 'Cthulhu cult' phenomenon is not worship and gates nothing. Deliberate exclusions: (1) Living-author mythos entities (Ramsey Campbell's Glaaki, Y'golonac, Eihort; Brian Lumley's Cthulhu-cycle additions; Stephen King adjacents) were NOT listed at all — active copyright plus living authors puts them beyond even the modern_ip_flagged tier; revisit only with licensing. (2) Trademark caution: 'Call of Cthulhu' as a GAME title is a Chaosium trademark, and TTRPG-original entities (e.g. Chaosium-invented gods) were excluded; the underlying pre-1930 fiction is PD. (3) D-4: the real authors (Lovecraft, Derleth, Smith, Bierce, Chambers, Long, Barlow, Bishop) are historical humans, not entities; 'Lovecraft himself as a character' appears in later fiction but is story-only material. (4) Borderline dates honestly noted: owner's ruling keyed ip:public_domain_literary to 'pre-1928', but several core Lovecraft-solo works were published 1928–1936; all pre-1930 publications are confirmed US-PD as of 2026-01-01 and the 1930–37 solo works show no renewal records — I flagged all Lovecraft-solo material public_domain_literary with publication years stated in one_line so the owner can tighten the line if desired. (5) The character Abdul Alhazred and the 'Mad Arab' framing carry an Orientalist-caricature depiction risk worth an editorial pass, though nothing is restricted.

candidates.name: Slender Man
candidates.one_line: Faceless suited tall-thing of the 2009 Something Awful photo threads — the internet's first true god-tier boogeyman, who takes children into the trees.

candidates.name: The Backrooms
candidates.one_line: The place you noclip into — infinite damp yellow office rooms humming with fluorescent dread; a location so mythic it functions as an entity (and as a dungeon generator).

candidates.name: SCP-173 (The Sculpture)
candidates.one_line: Concrete statue that snaps necks the instant you blink — the SCP Foundation's founding monster (text CC; its famous face is an unlicensed real sculpture, unusable).

candidates.name: SCP-682 (Hard-to-Destroy Reptile)
candidates.one_line: An immortal, all-hating reptile the Foundation ritually fails to kill — a boss whose myth IS the termination log.

candidates.name: SCP-096 (The Shy Guy)
candidates.one_line: Weeping pale giant who kills anyone, anywhere, who has ever seen its face — even in a photo.

candidates.name: SCP-049 (The Plague Doctor)
candidates.one_line: Courteous beaked doctor whose touch 'cures' you into a shambling corpse — convinced the Pestilence is still loose.

candidates.name: SCP-106 (The Old Man)
candidates.one_line: Corroded grinning elder who drags victims through walls into his rusting pocket-dimension to play with them.

candidates.name: SCP-999 (The Tickle Monster)
candidates.one_line: Benevolent orange slime-blob that cures depression by tickling — the SCP canon's one pure joy-being, ideal comic-relief spirit.

candidates.name: SCP-076 (Able)
candidates.one_line: An ancient warrior who resurrects from a black stone coffin to slaughter, again and again — the Foundation's tame apocalypse that keeps escaping.

candidates.name: SCP-914 (The Clockworks)
candidates.one_line: A vast clockwork machine that 'refines' anything fed into it — dial from Rough to Very Fine and gamble on what comes out.

candidates.name: The Smiler
candidates.one_line: Floating grin-and-eyes in the Backrooms' dark levels — the community wiki's signature entity, drawn to light and to hope.

candidates.name: Siren Head
candidates.one_line: Forty feet of mummified limbs topped with twin air-raid sirens that replay the voices of the missing — Trevor Henderson's actively-defended flagship.

candidates.name: Cartoon Cat
candidates.one_line: A 1930s rubber-hose cartoon cat wearing reality wrong, grinning out of abandoned malls — Henderson's other enforced icon.

candidates.name: The Rake
candidates.one_line: Hairless crouching gray thing from a 2005 anonymous thread that watches you sleep from the foot of the bed — the orphan-work apex predator.

candidates.name: Jeff the Killer
candidates.one_line: Burn-bleached grinning teen who whispers 'go to sleep' — collective-authored slasher whose infamous image has murky, rumor-haunted provenance.

candidates.name: Sonic.exe
candidates.one_line: The blood-eyed corrupted-cartridge demon of a thousand kids' nightmares — known creepypasta author PLUS Sega's character underneath: double-encumbered.

candidates.name: The Skin-Taker (Candle Cove)
candidates.one_line: Skeleton puppet from a kids' show that only the children could see — from Kris Straub's Candle Cove, already licensed once to television.

candidates.name: Laughing Jack
candidates.one_line: Monochrome killer clown from an abandoned imaginary friend's revenge arc — authored creepypasta with a known, credited creator (Snuffbomb).

candidates.name: Herobrine
candidates.one_line: The white-eyed miner who was never in the code — Minecraft's collectively-hallucinated ghost, disclaimed by Mojang yet inseparable from their trade dress.

candidates.name: Ben Drowned
candidates.one_line: The drowned boy haunting a cursed Majora's Mask cartridge — 'you shouldn't have done that'; creator Alex Hall known, Nintendo underneath.

candidates.name: Zalgo
candidates.one_line: H̷e̴ ̶C̷o̸m̶e̵s̷ — the eldritch corruption-god who bleeds through text itself; the closest thing internet folklore has to a chaos deity, but with a known creator (Dave Kelly, 2004).

candidates.name: RNGesus
candidates.one_line: The gamer folk-god of random number generation, propitiated before every loot roll — collective jokelore, no owner, and the family's only genuine luck deity: casino-floor native.

candidates.name: Momo
candidates.one_line: Bulging-eyed bird-woman of the 2018 WhatsApp 'challenge' hoax — the face is a real Japanese sculpture ('Mother Bird', Link Factory), destroyed but still copyrighted.

candidates.name: The Subject (Russian Sleep Experiment)
candidates.one_line: What fifteen days without sleep left behind the sealed door — anonymous-authored cold-war body horror, orphan work.

candidates.name: Skibidi Toilet (the Skibidi horde)
candidates.one_line: Singing heads-in-toilets at war with the camera-headed men — Gen Alpha's folk mythos, and the list's most radioactive IP: rights assigned to Invisible Narratives, creator-control dispute live as of July 2026.

candidates.name: Smile.jpg (Smile Dog)
candidates.one_line: The cursed image of a grinning husky you must 'spread the word' about or dream of forever — a chain-letter curse in file form; anonymous orphan work.

candidates.name: Polybius
candidates.one_line: The government arcade cabinet of 1981 Portland legend that induced amnesia and vanished — an urban myth about a machine; the legend itself is unowned, though the name has been opportunistically trademarked.

candidates.name: Loab
candidates.one_line: The hollow-cheeked woman who haunts latent space — the first AI-native cryptid, discovered and claimed by artist Steph Maj Swanson (2022).

candidates.name: This Man
candidates.one_line: 'Ever dream this man?' — the unibrowed face thousands supposedly dreamed; actually a 2008 guerrilla-marketing hoax by Andrea Natella, so authored, not folk.

closed_material_notes: Nothing here is culturally/religiously closed — the family has no living worship and no tradition-holder restrictions. Exclusions made on other binding grounds: (1) D-4 adjacency: the 2014 Waukesha Slender Man stabbing involves real, living minors and a real victim — reported here as context only, never content; the persistent rumor that Jeff the Killer's image derives from a real deceased person is unverified and excluded as a claim; Momo's attachment to a real child-harm panic is context, not content. (2) IP-grounds exclusions (fully authored web fiction rather than folklore, with clear single owners): Marble Hornets and its Operator/Masky/Hoody (Troy Wagner & Joseph DeLage, THAC), commercial mascot-horror games (FNAF's Freddy, Poppy Playtime's Huggy Wuggy) — these are licensed entertainment properties, not folklore, and were not censused. (3) Family-boundary exclusions: pre-internet folklore that merely circulates online (Bloody Mary, the Wendigo, Mothman, Black-Eyed Kids' hitchhiker ancestors) belongs to other tradition families and was left to them. (4) SCP-173's original image (Izumi Kato's sculpture 'Untitled 2004') is excluded from any use: it was never CC-licensed and the SCP Wiki itself removed it in 2022 — the entity is text-only material.
